parts.content: Discuss how the randomness of radioactive decay supports the concept of probability in physics and its relevance to nuclear safety.
parts.markscheme:
- Radioactive decay is a random process $\implies$ cannot predict when a particular nucleus will decay <marks>1 mark</marks>
- However, the behavior of a large number of nuclei can be predicted using probability (law of large numbers) <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>
Award 1 mark for each relevant point relating randomness of radioactive decay to probability and its relevance to nuclear safety.
</note>

steps.title: Unpredictability of single nuclei
steps.content: At the microscopic level, radioactive decay is a fundamentally **random** and **spontaneous** process. This means that for any individual nucleus in a sample, it is impossible to predict exactly *when* it will decay. There is no physical way to "look" at a nucleus and determine its remaining lifespan; we only know it has a certain constant probability of decaying per unit time.

highlights.text: Radioactive decay is a random process
cannot predict when a particular nucleus will decay

steps.title: The Statistical Ensemble Approach
steps.content:
While we can't predict the fate of one nucleus, we can make extremely precise predictions about a large collection of nuclei, known as a **statistical ensemble**. 

According to the **law of large numbers**, as the number of nuclei ($N$) becomes very large, the actual fraction of nuclei decaying approaches the theoretical probability. This allows us to transition from microscopic uncertainty to macroscopic certainty, describing the decay of the entire sample with deterministic laws, such as the exponential decay law.

highlights.text: behavior of a large number of nuclei can be predicted using probability

steps.title: Impact on nuclear safety
steps.content:
This transition is critical for nuclear safety. Because we can predict the behavior of the ensemble (the macroscopic sample), safety engineers can accurately calculate:

1.  **Activity levels:** How many decays occur per second (Becquerels).
2.  **Radiation dose:** How much energy will be deposited in biological tissue over time.
3.  **Shielding requirements:** How much material is needed to reduce exposure to safe levels.

Without this statistical predictability, managing the risks of radioactive waste or nuclear power would be impossible because we wouldn't know the intensity of the radiation being emitted.

steps.title: Understand individual randomness
steps.content: In nuclear physics, radioactive decay is fundamentally a **random and spontaneous** process. This means that for any single, specific nucleus, it is impossible to predict exactly when it will decay. There is no physical change in a nucleus that signals it is "about to" decay; it simply happens without warning.

highlights.text: cannot predict when a particular nucleus will decay

steps.title: Define the decay constant
steps.content:
To manage this randomness, we use **Probabilistic Risk Modeling**. We define the decay constant, $\lambda$, as the **probability of decay per unit time** for a single nucleus. 

Even though we can't predict the outcome for one atom, this constant gives us a mathematical way to describe the risk (the likelihood) of decay occurring within a certain timeframe.

steps.title: Apply the law of large numbers
steps.content:
When we deal with a macroscopic sample (containing billions of nuclei), the individual randomness averages out. This is known as the **Law of Large Numbers**. 

The total activity ($A$), which is the number of decays per second, becomes a highly predictable value: 

$$A = \lambda N$$

Where $N$ is the number of nuclei. This transition from individual uncertainty to collective certainty is the foundation of probability in physics.

highlights.text: behavior of a large number of nuclei can be predicted

steps.title: Link to nuclear safety
steps.content:
This predictability is vital for **nuclear safety**. Because we can accurately calculate the activity of a source over time, engineers can:
1. Determine the exact amount of **shielding** required to protect workers.
2. Calculate how long nuclear waste must be stored before it reaches safe levels.
3. Establish safety zones around reactors based on the statistical risk of exposure.

Without this probabilistic modeling, we would have no way to quantify or manage the risks associated with radioactive materials.

steps.title: Unpredictability of individual events
steps.content:
In nuclear physics, radioactive decay is fundamentally a **random process**. This means that if we look at a single radioactive nucleus, it is impossible to predict exactly *when* it will decay. 

There is no way to determine the "age" of a nucleus or how much longer it will last; we can only assign a constant **probability** that it will decay within a certain time interval.

highlights.text: Radioactive decay is a random process

steps.title: Graphical predictability of aggregates
steps.content:
While individual events are unpredictable, the **Graphical Interpretation** approach shows that for a large number of nuclei, the "noise" of randomness averages out. 

When we plot the number of remaining nuclei ($N$) over time ($t$), we get a smooth, predictable **exponential decay curve**. This is an application of the law of large numbers: the collective behavior of millions of random events follows a strict mathematical trend, typically represented by:

$$N = N_0 e^{-\lambda t}$$

On a graph, this appears as a smooth curve where the activity halves every half-life, allowing us to predict the state of the material with high precision.

calculator.description: Calculate the remaining percentage of a sample after 3 half-lives to show predictability.

steps.title: Relevance to nuclear safety
steps.content:
This transition from individual randomness to collective predictability is vital for **nuclear safety**. 

Because the decay curve is smooth and predictable, engineers can precisely calculate:
1. How much shielding is required at a specific time.
2. How long nuclear waste must be stored before it reaches safe levels.
3. The exact risk levels for workers based on the calculated activity of a source.

Without this statistical predictability, managing the long-term risks of radioactive materials would be impossible.

highlights.text: behavior of a large number of nuclei can be predicted using probability

parts.content: State the relationship between the activity and the number of undecayed nuclei. Explain the implication of this relationship for the power output of a radioactive source over time.
parts.markscheme:
- The activity $A$ is proportional to the number of undecayed nuclei $N$ ($A = \lambda N$, where $\lambda$ is the decay constant) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- As nuclei decay, $N$ decreases, causing the activity (and thus power output) to decrease over time <marks>1 mark</marks>
- This necessitates periodic replacement or refueling of the source to maintain energy production (e.g. in RTGs or medical applications) <marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: State the Proportionality
steps.content:
The activity ($A$) of a radioactive sample represents the number of nuclei decaying per second. This activity is directly proportional to the number of undecayed nuclei ($N$) currently present in the sample. This is expressed by the equation:

$$A = \lambda N$$

where $\lambda$ is the decay constant, representing the probability of decay per unit time for a specific nuclide.

steps.title: Analyze the Decay Process
steps.content: Radioactive decay is a continuous process. As time passes, the undecayed nuclei ($N$) transform into stable (or different) daughter nuclei. Because these nuclei are "used up" during the process, the total number of undecayed nuclei $N$ must decrease over time.

steps.title: Link to Power Output
steps.content:
Since activity is proportional to $N$ ($A \propto N$), a decrease in the number of nuclei leads to a corresponding decrease in activity. 

The power output of a radioactive source depends on the energy released per decay and the frequency of those decays (activity). Therefore, as the activity drops, the power output of the source also decreases over time.

steps.title: Identify Practical Implications
steps.content: Because the power output is not constant, it will eventually fall below the level required to operate a device or perform a medical treatment. In practice, this means that radioactive sources in technology—such as those used in space probes or medical equipment—must be periodically replaced or refueled to maintain a steady and reliable energy supply.

steps.title: Define the mathematical relationship
steps.content:
The activity $A$ of a radioactive sample is the rate at which nuclei decay. It is directly proportional to the number of undecayed nuclei $N$ currently present in the sample. This is expressed by the fundamental decay equation:

$$A = \lambda N$$

Where:
- $A$ is the activity (Bequerels, Bq)
- $N$ is the number of undecayed nuclei
- $\lambda$ is the decay constant, representing the probability of decay per unit time for a specific nuclide.

highlights.text: relationship between the activity and the number of undecayed nuclei

steps.title: Link activity to power
steps.content:
In a radioactive source (like those used in space probes or medical devices), each decay event releases a specific amount of energy. Since power $P$ is the rate of energy release, it is directly proportional to the activity $A$:

$$P \propto A$$

According to the **Exponential Decay Model**, the number of nuclei $N$ decreases exponentially over time ($N = N_0 e^{-\lambda t}$). Because $A = \lambda N$, the activity—and consequently the power output—must also follow a continuous exponential decline:

$$P(t) = P_0 e^{-\lambda t}$$

As nuclei are used up, there are fewer left to decay, meaning the energy produced per second inevitably drops.

gdc.instructions: Evaluate the exponential decay factor for t = 2 * T_1/2.

ti84.instructions: To find the remaining fraction after 2 half-lives, calculate 0.5 squared.

desmos.instructions: Enter the expression to see the remaining power ratio.

calculator.description: Calculate the remaining power percentage after two half-lives using the exponential model.

highlights.text: implication of this relationship for the power output

steps.title: Address practical implications
steps.content:
Because the power output is constantly decreasing, a radioactive power source cannot provide a steady level of energy indefinitely. This has a critical implication for safety and engineering:

To maintain a required level of energy production (e.g., to keep a satellite's instruments running or a medical device functional), the source must be **periodically replaced or refueled**. 

If the power drops below a certain threshold, the system may fail or become unsafe, necessitating strict maintenance schedules based on the known half-life of the isotope used.

parts.content: Explain how the random nature of radioactive decay impacts safety and storage considerations for nuclear materials.
parts.markscheme:
- Radioactive decay is random and spontaneous, meaning the emission of radiation cannot be stopped or controlled <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Therefore, continuous shielding and containment must be maintained to protect against radiation <marks>1 mark</marks>
- However, for large samples, the activity is predictable (using half-life), allowing the calculation of safe storage times <marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Understand randomness and spontaneity
steps.content:
At the microscopic level, radioactive decay is **random** and **spontaneous**. This means that it is impossible to predict exactly when a specific nucleus will decay, and the process cannot be started, stopped, or even slowed down by external factors like temperature or pressure.

In an IB exam, identifying that the process is **uncontrollable** is the first key to earning marks for this concept.

steps.title: Address safety and shielding
steps.content: Because we cannot control or "turn off" the emission of radiation, nuclear materials are constantly emitting high-energy particles or photons. To manage this risk, **continuous physical shielding** (such as thick lead or concrete) and secure containment must be maintained at all times to protect people and the environment from exposure.

steps.title: Link to macroscopic predictability
steps.content:
While individual decays are unpredictable, a large sample of radioactive material follows a predictable statistical pattern. This is characterized by the **half-life**—the time it takes for half of the radioactive nuclei in a sample to decay.

This macroscopic predictability allows scientists and engineers to calculate the **activity** of a sample over long periods.

steps.title: Calculate safe storage times
steps.content: By using the half-life, we can determine exactly how long nuclear waste must be stored before its radioactivity levels drop enough to be considered safe. This predictability is essential for the design and long-term planning of storage facilities, such as deep geological repositories, which may need to remain secure for thousands of years.

questions.specification: A laboratory investigates decay chains in naturally occurring uranium-238, which decays via a series of $\alpha$ and $\beta^-$ decays to stable $^{206}_{82}\text{Pb}$. One intermediate nuclide is $^{234}_{90}\text{Th}$, which decays by $\beta^-$ emission with a half-life of 24.1 days.

parts.content: Explain how the decay chain conserves both nucleon number and charge at each stage.
parts.markscheme:
- In each decay step:
 Mass number is conserved (no nucleons are lost overall) <marks>1</marks>
- Charge is conserved through transformation of protons/neutrons and emission of appropriate particles <marks>1</marks>

steps.title: Define conservation laws
steps.content:
To explain conservation in a decay chain, we must show that for every individual reaction, two quantities remain constant:
1. **Nucleon number ($A$):** The total number of protons and neutrons.
2. **Atomic number ($Z$):** The total electric charge (number of protons).

In the context of **Topic E.3 Radioactive decay**, this means the sum of $A$ and $Z$ on the reactant side must equal the sum of $A$ and $Z$ on the product side.

steps.title: Analyze Alpha ($\alpha$) decay
steps.content:
An alpha particle is a helium nucleus, represented as $^{4}_{2}\alpha$ or $^{4}_{2}\text{He}$. The general equation for $\alpha$ decay is:

$$^{A}_{Z}X \rightarrow ^{A-4}_{Z-2}Y + ^{4}_{2}\alpha$$

- **Nucleon conservation:** The total nucleons before is $A$. After, it is $(A-4) + 4 = A$.
- **Charge conservation:** The total charge before is $Z$. After, it is $(Z-2) + 2 = Z$.

Both quantities are conserved in this step.

steps.title: Analyze Beta-minus ($\beta^-$) decay
steps.content:
In $\beta^-$ decay, a neutron turns into a proton, emitting an electron ($^{0}_{-1}\beta$) and an electron antineutrino ($^{0}_{0}\bar{\nu}_e$). The general equation is:

$$^{A}_{Z}X \rightarrow ^{A}_{Z+1}Y + ^{0}_{-1}\beta + ^{0}_{0}\bar{\nu}_e$$

- **Nucleon conservation:** The total nucleons before is $A$. After, it is $A + 0 + 0 = A$.
- **Charge conservation:** The total charge before is $Z$. After, it is $(Z+1) + (-1) + 0 = Z$.

Even though particles change identity, the totals remain the same.

steps.title: Conclusion for the chain
steps.content:
Because every individual $\alpha$ and $\beta^-$ step in the decay chain from Uranium-238 to Lead-206 satisfies these equations, the **total nucleon number** and **total charge** are conserved throughout the entire process.

To earn the marks, ensure you state that:
- The mass number (nucleon number) is conserved as no nucleons are lost to the surroundings.
- The charge is conserved as the transformation of neutrons and protons is balanced by the emitted particles.

highlights.text: Mass number is conserved (no nucleons are lost overall)
Charge is conserved through transformation of protons/neutrons and emission of appropriate particles

steps.title: Identify the conservation laws
steps.content:
To explain conservation in nuclear reactions, we look at the two quantities that must remain the same before and after the decay: 
1. **Nucleon number ($A$):** The total number of protons and neutrons.
2. **Charge ($Z$):** The total electrical charge, usually represented by the number of protons (atomic number).

We will examine how the physical processes of $\alpha$ and $\beta^-$ decay maintain these values.

steps.title: Analyze alpha decay process
steps.content:
In an alpha ($\alpha$) decay, the nucleus ejects a discrete package consisting of **2 protons and 2 neutrons** (a Helium-4 nucleus).

*   **Nucleon conservation:** Since 4 nucleons are removed from the nucleus and carried away by the $\alpha$ particle, the total number of nucleons in the system (daughter nucleus + $\alpha$ particle) remains exactly the same as the parent nucleus.
*   **Charge conservation:** The nucleus loses a charge of $+2e$. However, the emitted $\alpha$ particle carries this $+2e$ charge with it. Therefore, the total charge before the decay equals the total charge after.

steps.title: Analyze beta-minus decay process
steps.content:
In a beta-minus ($\beta^-$) decay, a **neutron** inside the nucleus transforms into a **proton**, an **electron**, and an antineutrino.

*   **Nucleon conservation:** Because a neutron ($A=1$) is replaced by a proton ($A=1$), the total number of nucleons ($A$) within the nucleus does not change. The emitted electron has a nucleon number of 0, so the total $A$ is conserved.
*   **Charge conservation:** A neutral neutron (charge 0) becomes a positive proton (charge $+1e$) and a negative electron (charge $-1e$). 

$$\text{Total charge after} = (+1e) + (-1e) = 0$$

Since the starting charge of the neutron was 0, the total charge is conserved during this transformation.

steps.title: Summarize the findings
steps.content:
By considering these physical transformations, we can conclude:
1. **Nucleon number is conserved** because no nucleons are created or destroyed; they are either relocated (in $\alpha$ decay) or change type while maintaining a nucleon count of 1 (in $\beta^-$ decay).
2. **Charge is conserved** because the sum of the charges of the resulting particles (daughter nucleus and emitted radiation) always equals the charge of the original parent nucleus.

parts.content: Write an equation for the $\beta^-$ decay of $^{234}_{90}\text{Th}$.
parts.markscheme: $^{234}_{90}\text{Th} \rightarrow \, ^{234}_{91}\text{Pa} + \beta^- + \bar{\nu}_e$ <marks>1</marks>

steps.title: Identify the parent nucleus
steps.content:
In this problem, we are starting with the isotope Thorium-234. This is our 'parent' nucleus that will undergo decay. We are told it undergoes $\beta^-$ (beta-minus) emission.

From the question, we have:
- Parent Nucleus: $^{234}_{90}\text{Th}$
- Decay Type: $\beta^-$ emission

steps.title: Conserve the nucleon number
steps.content:
In any nuclear reaction, the total nucleon number ($A$), which is the sum of protons and neutrons, must be conserved. 

During $\beta^-$ decay, a neutron in the nucleus converts into a proton. Since both neutrons and protons are nucleons, the total number of nucleons does not change.

$$A_{\text{parent}} = A_{\text{daughter}} + A_{\text{beta}} + A_{\text{neutrino}}$$
$$234 = A_{\text{daughter}} + 0 + 0$$
$$A_{\text{daughter}} = 234$$

steps.title: Conserve the atomic number
steps.content:
Next, we conserve the atomic number ($Z$), which represents the total charge. 

In $\beta^-$ decay, a neutron ($0$ charge) becomes a proton ($+1$ charge) and emits an electron ($-1$ charge). The daughter nucleus must have one more proton than the parent to balance the negative charge of the emitted electron.

$$\begin{aligned} Z_{\text{parent}} &= Z_{\text{daughter}} + Z_{\text{beta}} \\ 90 &= Z_{\text{daughter}} + (-1) \\ Z_{\text{daughter}} &= 90 + 1 \\ Z_{\text{daughter}} &= 91 \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the atomic number of the daughter nucleus.

steps.title: Identify the daughter element
steps.content: An atomic number of $Z = 91$ corresponds to the element Protactinium (Pa). Combining this with our nucleon number $A = 234$, the daughter nucleus is $^{234}_{91}\text{Pa}$.

steps.title: Include the antineutrino
steps.content: To satisfy the law of **lepton number conservation**, every $\beta^-$ decay (where an electron/lepton is created) must also produce an electron antineutrino ($\bar{\nu}_e$). The antineutrino has no charge and negligible mass, so it doesn't change the $A$ or $Z$ balance.

steps.title: Final decay equation
steps.content:
Putting all the components together, we write the complete nuclear equation. Note that the beta particle is often written as $\beta^-$ or $^0_{-1}e$.

$$^{234}_{90}\text{Th} \rightarrow \, ^{234}_{91}\text{Pa} + \beta^- + \bar{\nu}_e$$

This equation earns the full mark for showing the correct daughter nucleus, the electron, and the antineutrino.

parts.content: Determine the activity of a sample containing $6.25 \times 10^{18}$ nuclei of $^{234}_{90}\text{Th}$.
parts.markscheme:
- $T_{1/2} = 24.1 \times 86400 = 2.08 \times 10^6 \, \text{s}$ <marks>1</marks>
- $\lambda = \frac{\ln 2}{T_{1/2}} \approx 3.33 \times 10^{-7} \, \text{s}^{-1}$
- $A = \lambda N = 3.33 \times 10^{-7} \times 6.25 \times 10^{18} \approx 2.08 \times 10^{12} \, \text{Bq}$ <marks>1</marks>

steps.title: Convert half-life to seconds
steps.content:
To calculate the activity in Becquerels (Bq), which is decays per second, we must first convert the half-life from days into seconds.

There are 24 hours in a day, 60 minutes in an hour, and 60 seconds in a minute ($24 \times 60 \times 60 = 86400$ seconds per day).

$$T_{1/2} = 24.1 \text{ days} \times 86400 \text{ s/day}$$
$$T_{1/2} = 2,082,240 \text{ s}$$

In scientific notation, this is approximately $2.08 \times 10^6 \text{ s}$.

gdc.instructions: Enter 24.1 * 24 * 60 * 60 into the main calculation screen.

desmos.instructions: Type 24.1 * 86400 into a new expression line.

calculator.description: Convert days to seconds.

steps.title: Calculate the decay constant
steps.content:
The decay constant ($\lambda$) represents the probability of decay per unit time. It is related to the half-life ($T_{1/2}$) by the following formula found in **Topic E.3 Radioactive Decay**:

$$\lambda = \frac{\ln 2}{T_{1/2}}$$

Substituting our value for the half-life:

$$\lambda = \frac{\ln 2}{2,082,240 \text{ s}}$$
$$\lambda \approx 3.3288 \times 10^{-7} \text{ s}^{-1}$$

gdc.instructions: Use the ln(2) function divided by 2082240.

calculator.description: Calculate the decay constant using the natural log of 2.

steps.title: Determine the sample activity
steps.content:
The activity ($A$) of a sample is the rate at which nuclei decay. It is calculated by multiplying the decay constant ($\lambda$) by the number of nuclei ($N$) present:

$$A = \lambda N$$

Using the given value for $N$:

$$A = (3.3288 \times 10^{-7} \text{ s}^{-1}) \times (6.25 \times 10^{18})$$
$$A \approx 2.0805 \times 10^{12} \text{ Bq}$$

gdc.instructions: Multiply the decay constant result by 6.25E18.

ti84.instructions: Multiply your previous answer by 6.25e18. (Use [2nd] [,] for 'e').

calculator.description: Multiply the decay constant by the number of nuclei.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
Rounding to three significant figures (to match the precision of the given half-life and number of nuclei), we get:

$$A = 2.08 \times 10^{12} \text{ Bq}$$

In the markscheme, 1 mark is awarded for correctly converting the half-life and another mark is awarded for calculating the final activity value with the correct units (Becquerels).

parts.content: Calculate the time for the activity to fall to one-third of its original value.
parts.markscheme:
- $A = A_0 e^{-\lambda t} \implies \frac{1}{3} = e^{-\lambda t} \implies \ln 3 = \lambda t$ <marks>1</marks>
- $t = \frac{\ln 3}{\lambda} = \frac{1.0986}{3.33 \times 10^{-7}} \approx 3.3 \times 10^6 \, \text{s}$ (approx $38.2$ days) <marks>1</marks>

steps.title: Identify given values
steps.content:
To solve this, we first identify the key information from the question:
*   The half-life of Thorium-234 is $$T_{1/2} = 24.1 \text{ days}$$.
*   We need to find the time $t$ when the activity $A$ falls to one-third of the initial activity $A_0$, which means $$A = \frac{1}{3}A_0$$.

This problem requires concepts from **Topic E.3: Radioactive decay**, specifically the quantitative analysis of decay.

steps.title: Calculate the decay constant
steps.content:
First, we calculate the decay constant $\lambda$ using the relationship between half-life and the decay constant:

$$\lambda = \frac{\ln 2}{T_{1/2}}$$

To find the answer in seconds (SI units), we first convert the half-life to seconds:
$$T_{1/2} = 24.1 \times 24 \times 60 \times 60 = 2,082,240 \text{ s}$$

Now, calculate $\lambda$:
$$\lambda = \frac{\ln 2}{2,082,240} \approx 3.33 \times 10^{-7} \text{ s}^{-1}$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate ln(2) divided by the product of 24.1 and 86400.

calculator.description: Calculate the decay constant λ in s⁻¹.

steps.title: Set up the decay equation
steps.content:
The activity $A$ at any time $t$ is given by the exponential decay law:

$$A = A_0 e^{-\lambda t}$$

We substitute the condition $A = \frac{1}{3}A_0$ into the equation:

$$\frac{1}{3}A_0 = A_0 e^{-\lambda t}$$

Dividing both sides by $A_0$ gives:

$$\frac{1}{3} = e^{-\lambda t}$$

steps.title: Solve for time t
steps.content:
To solve for $t$, we take the natural logarithm $(\ln)$ of both sides:

$$\ln\left(\frac{1}{3}\right) = -\lambda t$$

Since $\ln(1/3) = -\ln(3)$, the equation simplifies to:

$$\ln 3 = \lambda t$$

Now, rearrange for $t$:

$$t = \frac{\ln 3}{\lambda}$$

steps.title: Calculate the final answer
steps.content:
Finally, substitute the values of $\ln 3$ and $\lambda$ into the equation:

$$t = \frac{1.0986}{3.33 \times 10^{-7} \text{ s}^{-1}} \approx 3.30 \times 10^6 \text{ s}$$

Alternatively, calculating in days:
$$t = \frac{\ln 3}{\ln 2} \times 24.1 \text{ days} \approx 38.2 \text{ days}$$

gdc.instructions: Evaluate the expression for time t.

ti84.instructions: To find seconds: ln(3) / 3.33E-7. To find days: (ln(3)/ln(2)) * 24.1.

calculator.description: Calculate the final time in seconds and days.

steps.title: Identify given values
steps.content:
To solve this, we first note the given half-life of the thorium nuclide and the target activity level.

*   Half-life ($T_{1/2}$) = $24.1 \text{ days}$
*   Final Activity ($A$) = $\frac{1}{3}$ of the original activity ($A_0$)

steps.title: Apply powers of half-life
steps.content:
A useful way to express radioactive decay is using the "powers of half-life" formula:

$$A = A_0 \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^n$$

Where $n$ is the number of half-lives that have passed, defined as:

$$n = \frac{t}{T_{1/2}}$$

Substituting $A = \frac{1}{3}A_0$, we get:

$$\frac{1}{3} = \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{\frac{t}{24.1}}$$

steps.title: Solve using logarithms
steps.content:
To find the time $t$, we need to solve for the exponent. We do this by taking the natural logarithm ($\ln$) of both sides:

$$\ln\left(\frac{1}{3}\right) = \frac{t}{24.1} \ln\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)$$

Using the log property $\ln(1/x) = -\ln(x)$:

$$-\ln(3) = \frac{t}{24.1} (-\ln(2))$$

Rearranging for $t$:

$$t = 24.1 \times \frac{\ln(3)}{\ln(2)}$$

steps.title: Calculate the time
steps.content:
Now we calculate the numerical value. 

$$t = 24.1 \times \frac{1.0986}{0.6931} \approx 38.2 \text{ days}$$

To match the SI units often used in markschemes, we can convert this to seconds:

$$t = 38.2 \text{ days} \times 24 \text{ hours/day} \times 3600 \text{ seconds/hour} \approx 3.3 \times 10^6 \text{ s}$$

gdc.instructions: Use the natural log function to compute the number of half-lives, then multiply by the half-life and conversion factor.

ti84.instructions: Enter the expression for time in days and multiply by 86400 (seconds in a day).

desmos.instructions: Evaluate the logarithmic ratio to find the time in days, then convert.

calculator.description: Calculating the total time in days and converting to seconds.

parts.content: Discuss how decay chains complicate radiation protection and isotope analysis in environmental studies.
parts.markscheme:
- Decay chains release multiple radiation types over time, requiring monitoring of both parent and daughter isotopes <marks>1</marks>
- Isotope analysis must distinguish overlapping decay signals, which complicates environmental tracking and dating <marks>1</marks>

steps.title: Understand the decay chain
steps.content:
In this problem, we are looking at a **decay chain**. Unlike a single radioactive event, a decay chain involves a sequence where a parent isotope (like $^{238}_{92}\text{U}$) decays into a daughter isotope ($^{234}_{90}\text{Th}$), which is itself radioactive and continues to decay. 

This process continues until a stable isotope, such as $^{206}_{82}\text{Pb}$, is reached. This complexity affects two main areas: **radiation protection** and **isotope analysis**.

highlights.text: decays via a series of $\alpha$ and $\beta^-$ decays to stable $^{206}_{82}\text{Pb}$

steps.title: Impact on radiation protection
steps.content:
From a safety and protection standpoint, decay chains are challenging because they don't produce just one type of radiation. 

Because the daughter isotopes are also radioactive, the total activity of a sample can actually increase over time or remain high for much longer than expected. Radiation protection must account for:
- **Multiple radiation types**: A chain might release alpha ($\alpha$), beta ($\beta^-$), and gamma ($\gamma$) radiation simultaneously.
- **Extended monitoring**: Safety protocols cannot just focus on the parent isotope; they must monitor all daughter products in the environment to ensure cumulative exposure stays within safe limits.

highlights.text: Decay chains release multiple radiation types over time, requiring monitoring of both parent and daughter isotopes

steps.title: Impact on isotope analysis
steps.content:
In environmental studies, scientists use isotopes to "date" samples or track the movement of materials. Decay chains complicate this because:
- **Overlapping signals**: It becomes difficult to distinguish whether the detected radiation is coming from the original parent or one of the many intermediate daughters.
- **Complex dating**: Calculating the age of a sample (like in Uranium-Lead dating) requires precise knowledge of the ratios of all isotopes in the chain. If some daughter isotopes escape the environment (like Radon gas), the "clock" is reset or skewed, making environmental tracking and dating much more difficult.

highlights.text: Isotope analysis must distinguish overlapping decay signals, which complicates environmental tracking and dating

steps.title: Final Summary
steps.content:
To earn full marks, your discussion should clearly separate these two categories:

1.  **Radiation Protection**: Focus on the need to monitor multiple types of radiation from both parents and daughters over time.
2.  **Isotope Analysis**: Focus on the difficulty of distinguishing overlapping signals, which complicates dating and environmental tracking.

steps.title: Monitoring Multiple Radiation Types
steps.content: In a decay chain, the parent isotope doesn't just disappear; it transforms into daughter isotopes that are themselves radioactive. Because these daughters often emit different types of radiation—such as the $\alpha$ decay from $^{238}\text{U}$ followed by the $\beta^-$ decay from $^{234}\text{Th}$—radiation protection must account for varying penetrating powers and ionization levels. This requires sophisticated monitoring of **both parent and daughter isotopes** to ensure safety, as the hazard profile changes over time.

highlights.text: monitoring of both parent and daughter isotopes

steps.title: Overlapping Signals and Half-lives
steps.content: The physical properties of isotopes in a chain, specifically their different half-lives (like the **half-life of 24.1 days** for $^{234}_{90}\text{Th}$), create a complex and changing radiation "signal." In environmental studies, this means that the detected radiation is a mixture of signals from multiple species at different stages of decay. Distinguishing these **overlapping decay signals** is a major challenge for isotope analysis, making it much harder to accurately track the movement of contaminants or perform carbon/uranium dating.

steps.title: Summary of Marks
steps.content:
To earn full marks on this question, you need to address two distinct points:
1.  **Multiple radiation types:** Mention that the presence of different decay products (daughters) necessitates monitoring more than just the original parent isotope.
2.  **Analysis complications:** Explain that the varying physical properties (half-lives) lead to overlapping signals, which makes tracking and dating difficult.

steps.title: Understanding Decay Dynamics
steps.content:
In a decay chain, a parent isotope doesn't just turn into a stable product in one step. Instead, it creates a sequence of daughter isotopes that are often radioactive themselves. Using the **Decay Dynamics Approach**, we look at how the total activity of a sample is a moving target: as the parent isotope (like $^{238}_{92}\text{U}$) decays, the concentration and activity of its daughters (like $^{234}_{90}\text{Th}$) change over time.

This behavior is covered in **Topic E.3: Radioactive Decay**, specifically focusing on how different isotopes coexist and decay at different rates.

steps.title: Complications in Radiation Protection
steps.content:
From a safety perspective, decay chains are difficult to manage because they release **multiple types of radiation** (alpha, beta, and gamma) simultaneously. Even if you effectively shield against the alpha particles from the parent, the daughter products may emit high-energy beta or gamma radiation. 

Therefore, radiation protection cannot just focus on one element; it requires constant monitoring of both parent and daughter isotopes to ensure the total dose remains within safe limits.

highlights.text: Decay chains release multiple radiation types over time, requiring monitoring of both parent and daughter isotopes

steps.title: Challenges in Isotope Analysis
steps.content:
In environmental studies, we often use isotope ratios to determine the age of a sample (like Uranium-Lead dating). However, decay dynamics make this tricky because:
1. **Overlapping Signals:** Different steps in the chain might emit similar radiation, making it hard to distinguish which isotope is responsible for a specific signal.
2. **Accumulation:** Daughter products accumulate and then decay further, meaning the "signal" we measure today is the result of many overlapping decay rates. 

This complexity makes it much harder to track environmental contaminants or calculate precise dates compared to a simple, single-step decay.

highlights.text: Isotope analysis must distinguish overlapping decay signals, which complicates environmental tracking and dating

labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: All parts focus on decay constant definition, half-life from data, converting mass to number of nuclei, activity at a given time, and comparing activities via different decay constants—core quantitative radioactive decay relationships (N(t), A=λN, t1/2).

parts.content: Define the decay constant, $\lambda$, for a radioactive substance.
parts.markscheme: - The probability of decay of a nucleus per unit time. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Using the data in the table, determine the half-life of Isotope-131 in seconds. Show your working.
parts.markscheme:
- Identify from table that mass halves every 8 days (e.g., $4.80$ to $2.40$) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Convert days to seconds: $8 \times 24 \times 3600 = 691,200$ s <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Calculate the initial number of nuclei, $N_0$, in the $4.80\ \mu\text{g}$ sample. (Molar mass of Isotope-131 is $131\ \text{g mol}^{-1}$; Avogadro constant $N_A = 6.02 \times 10^{23}\ \text{mol}^{-1}$)
parts.markscheme:
- Calculate moles: $n = \frac{4.80 \times 10^{-6}}{131} = 3.66 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{mol}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Calculate nuclei: $N_0 = n \times N_A = 3.66 \times 10^{-8} \times 6.02 \times 10^{23} = 2.20 \times 10^{16}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Determine the activity of the sample at $t = 16$ days. Give your answer in Becquerels (Bq).
parts.markscheme:
- Calculate decay constant: $\lambda = \frac{\ln 2}{691200} = 1.00 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{s}^{-1}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Calculate activity at 16 days ($2$ half-lives): $N = \frac{N_0}{4} = 5.51 \times 10^{15}$
- Use $A = \lambda N = (1.00 \times 10^{-6}) \times (5.51 \times 10^{15}) = 5.51 \times 10^9\ \text{Bq}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: A different isotope, Isotope-Y, has a decay constant that is exactly three times larger than that of Isotope-131. Compare the initial activity of a sample of Isotope-Y to that of Isotope-131, assuming both samples initially contain the same number of nuclei.
parts.markscheme:
- State the ratio: The activity of Isotope-Y will be three times the activity of Isotope-131 (ratio 3:1) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Explain reasoning: Since $A = \lambda N$, if $N$ is constant, activity is directly proportional to the decay constant <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: In nuclear medicine, isotopes with relatively short half-lives are preferred for use as internal tracers. Suggest one medical advantage and one logistical disadvantage of this choice.
parts.markscheme:
- Advantage: The patient is exposed to radiation for a shorter duration, reducing the total absorbed dose/long-term risk <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Disadvantage: The isotope must be produced and transported quickly, or manufactured on-site (e.g., using a cyclotron), due to its rapid decay <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification:
Thorium-232 ($^{232}_{90}\text{Th}$) can decay via alpha decay. The following data are given:

| Nuclide | Binding Energy per Nucleon (MeV) |
| :--- | :--- |
| $^{232}_{90}\text{Th}$ | $7.615$ |
| $^{4}_{2}\alpha$ | $7.074$ |

The decay releases $4.083\ \text{MeV}$ of energy. What is the binding energy per nucleon of the daughter nucleus?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic E - Nuclear and quantum physics > E.3 Radioactive decay > E.3.1 Stability and binding energy
labeledSubSubTopics.title: E.3.1 Stability and binding energy
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question uses binding energy per nucleon data and energy released (Q-value) in alpha decay to infer the daughter nucleus binding energy per nucleon, directly testing nuclear stability/binding energy concepts.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic E - Nuclear and quantum physics > E.3 Radioactive decay > E.3.2 Types of radioactive decay

labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The scenario is explicitly alpha decay of Th-232, so it involves identifying/using the properties of a specific type of radioactive decay (alpha emission).

options.markscheme:
**Identify** the daughter nucleus using nucleon number conservation:
$$ 232 = A + 4 \Rightarrow A = 228 $$

**Calculate** the total binding energy of the parent ($^{232}\text{Th}$) and the alpha particle ($^4\alpha$):
$$ \text{Total BE}_{\text{parent}} = 232 \times 7.615 = 1766.68\ \text{MeV} $$
$$ \text{Total BE}_{\alpha} = 4 \times 7.074 = 28.296\ \text{MeV} $$

**Use** conservation of energy ($Q = \text{BE}_{\text{products}} - \text{BE}_{\text{parent}}$):
$$ 4.083 = (\text{Total BE}_{\text{daughter}} + 28.296) - 1766.68 $$

**Solve** for the total binding energy of the daughter nucleus:
$$ \text{Total BE}_{\text{daughter}} = 1766.68 - 28.296 + 4.083 $$
$$ \text{Total BE}_{\text{daughter}} = 1742.467\ \text{MeV} $$

**Calculate** the binding energy per nucleon for the daughter nucleus:
$$ \frac{1742.467}{228} \approx 7.642\ \text{MeV} $$

questions.specification:
A student monitors the decay of a sample of Sodium-24 ($^{24}\text{Na}$), a radioisotope used in medical diagnostics. The activity $A$ of the sample is recorded in a table over a period of time.

| Time $t$ (hours) | Activity $A$ (kBq) |
| :--- | :--- |
| 0 | 480 |
| 15 | 240 |
| 30 | 120 |
| 45 | 60 |

(Molar mass of Sodium-24 = $24 \text{ g mol}^{-1}$; Avogadro constant $N_A = 6.02 \times 10^{23} \text{ mol}^{-1}$)

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic E - Nuclear and quantum physics > E.3 Radioactive decay > E.3.3 Quantitative analysis of decay (HL only)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: E.3.3 Quantitative analysis of decay (HL only)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Uses activity–time data to infer half-life, defines decay constant, calculates decay probability over a time interval, finds initial number of nuclei from activity (A=λN), and explains short-time statistical fluctuations—core quantitative radioactive decay.

parts.content: Define the decay constant $\lambda$.
parts.markscheme: - The probability of decay of a single nucleus per unit time <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: State the half-life of Sodium-24 based on the data provided in the table.
parts.markscheme: - Identify that the activity halves every 15 hours, so $t_{1/2} = 15 \text{ hours}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Determine the probability that a specific Sodium-24 nucleus in the sample will decay during the first hour of the experiment.
parts.markscheme:
- Calculate the decay constant in reciprocal hours: $\lambda = \frac{\ln 2}{15} \approx 0.0462 \text{ h}^{-1}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Calculate the probability $P = 1 - e^{-\lambda t} \approx 1 - e^{-0.0462 \times 1} \approx 0.045$ (or $4.5\%$) <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Calculate the initial number of undecayed Sodium-24 nuclei, $N_0$, in the sample at $t = 0$.
parts.markscheme:
- Convert half-life and activity to SI units: $\lambda = \frac{\ln 2}{15 \times 3600} \approx 1.28 \times 10^{-5} \text{ s}^{-1}$ and $A_0 = 4.8 \times 10^5 \text{ Bq}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Use $A = \lambda N$ to find $N_0$: $N_0 = \frac{4.8 \times 10^5}{1.28 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 3.75 \times 10^{10}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Determine the initial mass of the Sodium-24 present in the sample.
parts.markscheme:
- Use moles = $\frac{N_0}{N_A}$: $n = \frac{3.75 \times 10^{10}}{6.02 \times 10^{23}} \approx 6.23 \times 10^{-14} \text{ mol}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Calculate mass = $n \times 24 \text{ g mol}^{-1} \approx 1.5 \times 10^{-12} \text{ g}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain why the activity measured over a very short time interval (e.g., 1 second) might show significant fluctuations compared to the predicted values in the table.
parts.markscheme:
- State that radioactive decay is a random/stochastic process <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Explain that for small time intervals or small numbers of decays, the statistical variations (fluctuations) become more apparent relative to the mean rate <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: State what is meant by the term half-life.
parts.markscheme: The time taken for half of the radioactive nuclei in a sample to decay <marks> 1 mark </marks>

parts.content: Calculate the decay constant of this isotope.
parts.markscheme:
- Use $\lambda = \frac{\ln 2}{T_{1/2}}$ <marks> 1 mark </marks>


- $\lambda = \frac{0.693}{600\ \text{s}} \approx 1.16 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{s}^{-1}$ <marks> 1 mark </marks>

steps.title: Identify given half-life
steps.content: The question provides the half-life ($T_{1/2}$) of the radioactive isotope. This is the time taken for the activity or the number of nuclei to halve.

steps.title: Convert time to seconds
steps.content:
To calculate the decay constant in standard SI units ($\text{s}^{-1}$), we must first convert the half-life from minutes into seconds.

$$\begin{aligned} T_{1/2} &= 10\text{ minutes} \\ T_{1/2} &= 10 \times 60\text{ s} \\ T_{1/2} &= 600\text{ s} \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Convert 10 minutes to seconds.

steps.title: Apply decay constant formula
steps.content:
We use the direct relationship between the decay constant ($\lambda$) and the half-life ($T_{1/2}$) from the **Nuclear and quantum physics** topic of your studies (Topic E.3).

$$\lambda = \frac{\ln 2}{T_{1/2}}$$

Substituting our known values:

$$\lambda = \frac{\ln 2}{600\text{ s}}$$

steps.title: Calculate the final value
steps.content:
Now, perform the division. Using the approximate value $\ln 2 \approx 0.693$:

$$\begin{aligned} \lambda &= \frac{0.693147...}{600} \\ \lambda &\approx 1.1552 \times 10^{-3}\text{ s}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

Rounding to three significant figures, we get:

$$\lambda = 1.16 \times 10^{-3}\text{ s}^{-1}$$

gdc.instructions: Use the natural log function: ln(2) / 600.

desmos.instructions: Type ln(2) / 600 into the input field.

calculator.description: Calculate the decay constant.

steps.title: Convert time to seconds
steps.content:
To calculate the decay constant in standard SI units ($\text{s}^{-1}$), we first convert the half-life ($T_{1/2}$) from minutes to seconds.

$$T_{1/2} = 10 \text{ minutes} \times 60 \text{ s/min} = 600 \text{ s}$$

steps.title: Apply exponential decay law
steps.content:
The exponential decay law describes how the number of nuclei $N$ changes over time $t$:

$$N = N_0 e^{-\lambda t}$$

Where:
- $N_0$ is the initial number of nuclei.
- $\lambda$ is the decay constant.
- $t$ is the elapsed time.

steps.title: Substitute half-life conditions
steps.content:
By definition, after one half-life ($t = T_{1/2} = 600 \text{ s}$), the number of remaining nuclei is half of the original amount ($N = 0.5 N_0$). Substituting these into the decay law:

$$0.5 N_0 = N_0 e^{-\lambda(600)}$$

Dividing both sides by $N_0$ simplifies the equation to:

$$0.5 = e^{-600\lambda}$$

steps.title: Solve for decay constant
steps.content:
To solve for $\lambda$, we take the natural logarithm ($\ln$) of both sides:

$$\begin{aligned} \ln(0.5) &= \ln(e^{-600\lambda}) \\ \ln(0.5) &= -600\lambda \end{aligned}$$

Rearranging for $\lambda$:

$$\lambda = \frac{\ln(0.5)}{-600}$$

steps.title: Calculate final value
steps.content:
Using the identity $\ln(0.5) = -\ln(2)$, we get:

$$\lambda = \frac{-\ln(2)}{-600} = \frac{\ln(2)}{600}$$

Calculating the numerical value:

$$\lambda \approx 1.16 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s}^{-1}$$

This matches the expected markscheme result and demonstrates the relationship between the decay constant and half-life.

gdc.instructions: Enter ln(2) divided by 600 in the main calculation screen.

ti84.instructions: Press [ln], enter 2, press [)], then press [/] followed by 600. Press [ENTER].

desmos.instructions: Type ln(2)/600 into the input bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the decay constant using the natural logarithm of 2 and the half-life in seconds.

parts.content: Determine the fraction of nuclei remaining after 30 minutes.
parts.markscheme:
- After 30 minutes = 3 half-lives → $N = N_0 \left( \frac{1}{2} \right)^3 = \frac{1}{8}$ <marks> 1 mark </marks>


- Fraction remaining = 0.125 <marks> 1 mark </marks>

steps.title: Identify the initial state
steps.content:
To solve this using the iterative halving approach, we start with the full amount of the isotope at $t = 0$. We represent the initial fraction as $1$.

According to the question, the isotope has a **half-life of 10 minutes**. This means every 10 minutes, the amount of radioactive nuclei remaining will be reduced by half.

steps.title: First 10-minute interval
steps.content:
After the first 10 minutes (one half-life), the initial fraction is halved:

$$\text{Fraction after 10 mins} = 1 \times \frac{1}{2} = \frac{1}{2}$$

steps.title: Second 10-minute interval
steps.content:
After another 10 minutes (total time = 20 minutes), the remaining fraction is halved again:

$$\text{Fraction after 20 mins} = \frac{1}{2} \times \frac{1}{2} = \frac{1}{4}$$

steps.title: Third 10-minute interval
steps.content:
Finally, after the last 10 minutes (total time = 30 minutes), we halve the fraction once more to reach our target time:

$$\text{Fraction after 30 mins} = \frac{1}{4} \times \frac{1}{2} = \frac{1}{8}$$

steps.title: State the final fraction
steps.content:
The question asks for the fraction remaining. We can express $\frac{1}{8}$ as a decimal.

$$\frac{1}{8} = 0.125$$

This matches the requirement for the second mark in the markscheme.

gdc.instructions: Input 1 divided by 8 into the main calculation screen.

calculator.description: Convert the final fraction to a decimal to match the markscheme.

steps.title: Identify given information
steps.content: To solve this, we first need to identify the total time elapsed and the length of one half-life from the question.

steps.title: Calculate number of half-lives
steps.content:
First, we calculate how many half-lives ($n$) have passed during the total time of $30$ minutes.

$$\begin{aligned} n &= \frac{\text{total time}}{\text{half-life}} \\ n &= \frac{30 \text{ min}}{10 \text{ min}} \\ n &= 3 \end{aligned}$$ 

This means the isotope has halved its remaining amount 3 times.

desmos.instructions: Type 30/10 in a new expression line.

calculator.description: Calculate the number of half-lives.

steps.title: Apply the fraction formula
steps.content:
Using the Power of Two Formula, the fraction of nuclei remaining is given by:

$$\text{Fraction remaining} = \left( \frac{1}{2} \right)^n$$

Substituting our value $n = 3$:

$$\text{Fraction remaining} = \left( \frac{1}{2} \right)^3$$

steps.title: Determine final fraction
steps.content:
Now we evaluate the power to find the final result:

$$\begin{aligned} \left( \frac{1}{2} \right)^3 &= \frac{1}{2} \times \frac{1}{2} \times \frac{1}{2} \\ &= \frac{1}{8} \end{aligned}$$

Converting this to a decimal, we get $0.125$. Both $\frac{1}{8}$ and $0.125$ are acceptable answers in IB Physics.

ti84.instructions: Enter (1/2)^3 and press ENTER. To convert to a fraction, press MATH, then 1 (Frac), then ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Type (1/2)^3 and toggle the fraction button on the left to see 1/8.

calculator.description: Evaluate the power of two formula.

steps.title: Identify given values
steps.content:
To solve this using the exponential decay equation, we first note the given values: the half-life ($T_{1/2}$) and the total time elapsed ($t$).

- Half-life $T_{1/2} = 10$ minutes
- Time $t = 30$ minutes

steps.title: Calculate the decay constant
steps.content:
The decay constant, $\lambda$, represents the probability of decay per unit time. It is related to the half-life by the following formula found in Topic E.3 (Radioactive Decay):

$$\lambda = \frac{\ln(2)}{T_{1/2}}$$

Substituting our half-life:

$$\lambda = \frac{\ln(2)}{10} \approx 0.0693 \text{ min}^{-1}$$

steps.title: Apply exponential decay law
steps.content:
The fraction of nuclei remaining, $\frac{N}{N_0}$, is given by the exponential decay law:

$$\frac{N}{N_0} = e^{-\lambda t}$$

Substituting $\lambda = \frac{\ln(2)}{10}$ and $t = 30$:

$$\frac{N}{N_0} = e^{-\left(\frac{\ln(2)}{10}\right) \times 30}$$

$$\frac{N}{N_0} = e^{-3\ln(2)}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter the expression exp(-(ln(2)/10)*30) into the main calculation screen.

ti84.instructions: Press [2nd] [ln] (to get e^), then type -((ln(2)/10)*30) and press [ENTER].

desmos.instructions: Type e^(-(ln(2)/10)*30) into the input line.

calculator.description: Calculate the remaining fraction using the exponential decay formula.

steps.title: Simplify and state answer
steps.content:
Using the property of logarithms $a\ln(b) = \ln(b^a)$:

$$\frac{N}{N_0} = e^{\ln(2^{-3})} = 2^{-3}$$

Calculating the value:

$$\frac{N}{N_0} = \frac{1}{2^3} = \frac{1}{8}$$

Converting to a decimal, we get:

$$\text{Fraction remaining} = 0.125$$

parts.content: Suggest why alpha particles are less penetrating than beta or gamma radiation.
parts.markscheme: Alpha particles have a large mass and charge, leading to strong interactions with matter and short range <marks> 1 mark </marks>

steps.title: Identify alpha particle properties
steps.content:
To understand why alpha particles have low penetration, we first look at their physical characteristics. An alpha particle ($
\alpha$) is a helium nucleus, consisting of two protons and two neutrons. Compared to beta particles (electrons) or gamma rays (photons), alpha particles have a **relatively large mass** and a **high positive charge** of $+2e$. 

This information relates to **Topic E.3: Radioactive decay**, where we study the properties of different types of radiation.

steps.title: Explain electromagnetic interactions
steps.content: Because alpha particles have a high charge ($+2e$), they exert strong electrostatic forces on the electrons of the atoms they pass through. This leads to a very high probability of interaction and frequent ionization of the surrounding matter. Unlike gamma radiation, which has no charge, the alpha particle's charge makes it 'clumsy' and much more likely to interact with every atom it encounters.

steps.title: Relate interactions to range
steps.content:
Due to their large mass and strong electromagnetic interactions, alpha particles lose their kinetic energy very quickly through these collisions and ionizations. This rapid energy loss results in a very short range in matter (usually only a few centimeters in air), making them significantly less penetrating than beta or gamma radiation.

To secure the mark, we simply need to link these physical properties to the outcome:

highlights.text: Alpha particles have a large mass and charge, leading to strong interactions with matter and short range

steps.title: Identify alpha particle properties
steps.content:
To understand why alpha particles have low penetration, we first look at their physical properties. An alpha particle is a helium nucleus, consisting of two protons and two neutrons. Compared to beta particles (electrons) or gamma rays (photons), alpha particles have a **large mass** and a **high positive charge** ($+2e$).

These properties are found in **Topic E.3 Radioactive decay** of your syllabus.

highlights.text: less penetrating than beta or gamma radiation

steps.title: Explain high ionizing power
steps.content:
Because of their large charge ($+2e$) and relatively slow speed, alpha particles interact strongly with the atoms in the material they are passing through. As they travel, they frequently pull electrons away from surrounding atoms, a process known as **ionization**.

From the physical constants in your data booklet, we know the elementary charge is:

$$e = 1.60 \times 10^{-19} \text{ C}$$

steps.title: Link ionization to energy loss
steps.content:
Each time an alpha particle ionizes an atom, it must do work to overcome the electrostatic attraction, which costs the particle some of its kinetic energy. Because they are so highly ionizing, they undergo many collisions and interactions in a very short distance.

$$\text{High Ionizing Power} \rightarrow \text{Rapid Kinetic Energy Loss}$$

steps.title: Conclude on penetration depth
steps.content: Since the alpha particle loses its kinetic energy so quickly over a short distance, it is easily stopped by simple barriers like a few centimeters of air or a thin sheet of paper. This explains why they are much less penetrating than beta or gamma radiation, which ionize matter much less frequently.

questions.specification:
Fluorine-18 ($^{18}_{9}\text{F}$) is a radioisotope that decays via positron ($\beta^+$) emission. 

Which row in the table correctly identifies the mass number ($A$) and the neutron number ($N$) of the nucleus produced by this decay?

| Option | Mass number ($A$) | Neutron number ($N$) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| A. | 18 | 9 |
| B. | 18 | 10 |
| C. | 17 | 8 |
| D. | 19 | 9 |

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic E - Nuclear and quantum physics > E.3 Radioactive decay > E.3.2 Types of radioactive decay
labeledSubSubTopics.title: E.3.2 Types of radioactive decay
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question specifically addresses the outcome of positron (̢+) emission, which is one of the primary types of radioactive decay categorized under this leaf topic.

options.markscheme: Incorrect. This assumes the atomic number and mass number remain unchanged, failing to account for the conversion of a proton into a neutron.

Correct. In positron ($\beta^+$) emission, a proton is converted into a neutron. The mass number ($A$) remains 18, but the atomic number ($Z$) decreases from 9 to 8. Therefore, the neutron number ($N = A - Z$) increases from 9 to 10.
Incorrect. This would suggest a loss of a proton and a neutron, similar to a partial alpha decay, which does not occur in $\beta^+$ emission.
Incorrect. This suggests an increase in mass number which violates the conservation laws of radioactive decay in this context.

parts.content: Explain the role of the strong nuclear force in the stability of the nucleus and describe why alpha decay occurs in certain nuclei.
parts.markscheme:
- The strong nuclear force holds protons and neutrons together in the nucleus, overcoming the electrostatic repulsion between protons <marks>1 mark</marks>

- In heavy nuclei, the strong nuclear force is insufficient to maintain stability due to high proton numbers, leading to alpha decay to increase stability <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Alpha decay reduces both the proton and neutron count, bringing the nucleus closer to a stable configuration <marks>1 mark</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: The Nuclear Tug-of-War
steps.content:
To understand nuclear stability, we must look at the competition between two fundamental forces acting within the nucleus. This concept is covered in **Topic E.3 (Radioactive decay)**. 

1.  **Electrostatic Repulsion**: Protons are positively charged and repel each other. According to Coulomb's law, this repulsive force acts over long distances within the nucleus.
2.  **Strong Nuclear Force (SNF)**: An extremely powerful attractive force that acts between all nucleons (protons-protons, neutrons-neutrons, and protons-neutrons).

steps.title: Role of the Strong Force
steps.content:
The primary role of the **strong nuclear force** is to provide the "glue" that holds the nucleus together. 

Because protons are packed so tightly, the electrostatic repulsion trying to push them apart is immense. The strong nuclear force must be significantly stronger than the electrostatic force at short distances to maintain a stable nucleus. 

Award yourself **1 mark** for stating that the strong nuclear force overcomes the electrostatic repulsion between protons to hold the nucleus together.

steps.title: The Range Problem
steps.content:
The key to the **Force Interaction Approach** is understanding the **range** of these forces:

*   **Electrostatic force** is long-range; every proton in a large nucleus repels every other proton.
*   **Strong nuclear force** is very short-range; a nucleon only feels the attraction from its immediate neighbors.

In heavy nuclei (those with many protons), the total repulsive force grows faster than the total attractive force because the nucleus becomes too large for the short-range strong force to reach across it. 

Award **1 mark** for explaining that in heavy nuclei, the strong force becomes insufficient to balance the high electrostatic repulsion.

steps.title: Alpha Decay and Stability
steps.content:
When the repulsive forces start to dominate, the nucleus becomes unstable. **Alpha decay** occurs as a way for the nucleus to reach a more stable, lower-energy state. 

By emitting an alpha particle ($^4_2\text{He}$), the nucleus loses 2 protons and 2 neutrons. This process:
*   Reduces the total number of protons, significantly decreasing the overall electrostatic repulsion.
*   Increases the relative strength of the strong nuclear force compared to the repulsion in the remaining smaller nucleus.

Award the final **1 mark** for noting that alpha decay reduces the nucleon count to bring the nucleus closer to a stable configuration.

steps.title: Force balance and binding
steps.content:
The nucleus is a busy place! Protons are all positively charged, so they naturally want to fly apart due to **electrostatic repulsion**. 

The **strong nuclear force** is what saves the day—it is a powerful, short-range attractive force that holds protons and neutrons (nucleons) together. The energy associated with this "glue" is called the **binding energy**. For a nucleus to be stable, the strong nuclear force must successfully overcome that electrostatic repulsion.

highlights.text: strong nuclear force holds protons and neutrons together in the nucleus, overcoming the electrostatic repulsion between protons

steps.title: Instability in heavy nuclei
steps.content:
As nuclei get larger (heavy nuclei), the number of protons increases. Because the strong nuclear force only acts over a very short range, it only attracts immediate neighbors. 

However, the electrostatic repulsion is long-range and acts across the entire nucleus. In very large nuclei, the total repulsion starts to win, and the strong nuclear force becomes **insufficient** to keep everything held tightly together. This makes the nucleus unstable.

highlights.text: strong nuclear force is insufficient to maintain stability due to high proton numbers

steps.title: Increasing stability via decay
steps.content:
To fix this instability, the nucleus undergoes **alpha decay**, where it spits out an alpha particle (2 protons and 2 neutrons). 

By losing these nucleons, the nucleus reduces its proton count and its overall size. Using the **binding energy approach**, we say the nucleus is trying to reach a more **stable configuration** with a higher **binding energy per nucleon**. This process moves the nucleus toward the "peak" of the binding energy curve, where nucleons are more tightly and efficiently bound.

highlights.text: Alpha decay reduces both the proton and neutron count, bringing the nucleus closer to a stable configuration

parts.content: Calculate the number of undecayed nuclei remaining after 9 hours.
parts.markscheme:
- Determine the number of half-lives:
  $$ t=9 \text { hours } \Rightarrow \frac{9}{3}=3 \text { half-lives } $$
  <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Calculate remaining nuclei:
  $$ N=N_0\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^3=1.0 \times 10^{20} \times \frac{1}{8}=1.25 \times 10^{19} $$
  <marks>1 mark</marks>

- State final answer: $1.25 \times 10^{19}$ (accept $1.3 \times 10^{19}$) nuclei remain after 9 hours <marks>1 mark</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Determine number of half-lives
steps.content:
A half-life is the time required for half of the radioactive nuclei in a sample to decay. First, we need to calculate how many half-life periods have occurred during the 9-hour interval.

$$\text{Number of half-lives } (n) = \frac{\text{Total time}}{\text{Half-life duration}}$$

$$n = \frac{9\text{ hours}}{3\text{ hours}} = 3 \text{ half-lives}$$

steps.title: Perform iterative halving
steps.content:
Using the iterative halving method, we start with the initial number of nuclei and divide the value by 2 for each half-life that passes. Since we have 3 half-lives, we will perform this division three times:

1.  **After 1st half-life (3 hours):**
    $$1.0 \times 10^{20} \div 2 = 5.0 \times 10^{19}$$
2.  **After 2nd half-life (6 hours):**
    $$5.0 \times 10^{19} \div 2 = 2.5 \times 10^{19}$$
3.  **After 3rd half-life (9 hours):**
    $$2.5 \times 10^{19} \div 2 = 1.25 \times 10^{19}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter 1.0*10^20, then divide by 2 three times.

desmos.instructions: Type the expression to calculate the final amount after 3 halvings.

calculator.description: Calculate the iterative division by 2.

steps.title: State final answer
steps.content:
After 9 hours (which corresponds to 3 half-lives), the number of undecayed nuclei remaining is $1.25 \times 10^{19}$. 

In IB Physics, rounding to appropriate significant figures is important. Since the input values were given to two significant figures, an answer of $1.3 \times 10^{19}$ is also acceptable.

steps.title: Identify given values
steps.content:
To solve this problem, we first identify the initial conditions and the time elapsed provided in the question:

*   Initial number of nuclei ($N_0$) = $1.0 \times 10^{20}$
*   Half-life ($T_{1/2}$) = $3$ hours
*   Total time elapsed ($t$) = $9$ hours

steps.title: Calculate number of half-lives
steps.content:
First, we need to find how many half-lives ($n$) have passed during the 9-hour period. This is found by dividing the total time by the duration of one half-life:

$$n = \frac{t}{T_{1/2}}$$

Substituting the values:

$$n = \frac{9 \text{ hours}}{3 \text{ hours}} = 3$$

This means the sample has halved exactly 3 times.

gdc.instructions: Enter the total time divided by the half-life duration.

ti84.instructions: Enter the total time divided by the half-life duration.

desmos.instructions: Enter the total time divided by the half-life duration.

calculator.description: Calculate the number of half-lives passed.

steps.title: Apply the power formula
steps.content:
We can now use the discrete decay formula to find the remaining undecayed nuclei ($N$):

$$N = N_0 \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^n$$

Substitute the known values into the equation:

$$N = (1.0 \times 10^{20}) \times \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^3$$

Since $(1/2)^3 = 1/8$:

$$N = (1.0 \times 10^{20}) \times 0.125$$

$$N = 1.25 \times 10^{19}$$

gdc.instructions: Multiply the initial nuclei by 0.5 raised to the power of the number of half-lives.

ti84.instructions: Multiply the initial nuclei by 0.5 raised to the power of the number of half-lives.

desmos.instructions: Multiply the initial nuclei by 0.5 raised to the power of the number of half-lives.

calculator.description: Calculate the final number of undecayed nuclei.

steps.title: State final answer
steps.content:
The number of undecayed nuclei remaining after 9 hours is:

$$N = 1.25 \times 10^{19} \text{ nuclei}$$

(Note: $1.3 \times 10^{19}$ is also acceptable if following 2 significant figures based on the input values).

steps.title: Identify known variables
steps.content:
First, let's list the information provided in the question. We are given the initial number of nuclei ($N_0$), the half-life ($T_{1/2}$), and the total time elapsed ($t$).

*   Initial nuclei: $N_0 = 1.0 \times 10^{20}$
*   Half-life: $T_{1/2} = 3$ hours
*   Time elapsed: $t = 9$ hours

This problem falls under **Topic E.3: Radioactive decay**, specifically focusing on the quantitative analysis of decay.

steps.title: Calculate the decay constant
steps.content:
The decay constant ($\lambda$) represents the probability of decay per unit time. We calculate it using the relationship between the half-life and the natural logarithm of 2:

$$\lambda = \frac{\ln(2)}{T_{1/2}}$$

Substituting our half-life of 3 hours:

$$\lambda = \frac{\ln(2)}{3} \approx 0.231 \text{ h}^{-1}$$

gdc.instructions: In the Run-Matrix menu, enter ln(2) / 3.

desmos.instructions: Type ln(2) / 3 into a blank cell.

calculator.description: Calculate the decay constant λ

steps.title: Apply the decay law
steps.content:
Now we use the **Radioactive Decay Law** equation to find the number of remaining nuclei ($N$) after 9 hours:

$$N = N_0 e^{-\lambda t}$$

Substituting our values:

$$N = (1.0 \times 10^{20}) \times e^{-(0.231 \times 9)}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the final number of nuclei N

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
Completing the calculation:

$$\begin{aligned} N &= 1.0 \times 10^{20} \times e^{-3 \ln(2)} \\ N &= 1.0 \times 10^{20} \times \frac{1}{8} \\ N &= 1.25 \times 10^{19} \end{aligned}$$

According to the markscheme, you receive 1 mark for identifying the decay proportion (effectively finding the number of half-lives), 1 mark for the calculation, and 1 mark for the correct final answer.

Final Answer: **$1.25 \times 10^{19}$ nuclei** (or $1.3 \times 10^{19}$ using two significant figures) remain undecayed.

parts.content: After the alpha decay, the daughter nucleus undergoes beta-minus decay. Describe the changes in the atomic and mass numbers of the nucleus following each decay.
parts.markscheme:
- Change after alpha decay: atomic number decreases by 2 and mass number decreases by 4 <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Change after beta-minus decay: atomic number increases by 1 (neutron converts to proton), mass number remains unchanged <marks>1 mark</marks>

- After both decays, final nucleus has atomic number $Z-1$ and mass number $A-4$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

<examTip>Make sure to clearly state both atomic and mass number changes for each decay type</examTip>

3 marks total

steps.title: Define the Initial State
steps.content:
To track the changes, let's represent the initial unstable nucleus as:

$${}^{A}_{Z}\text{X}$$

Where:
- $A$ is the **mass number** (total number of protons and neutrons).
- $Z$ is the **atomic number** (number of protons).

This question relates to **Topic E.3: Radioactive decay** in your syllabus. Understanding how the nucleus changes during decay is fundamental to this topic.

steps.title: Analyze Alpha Decay
steps.content:
In an **alpha decay**, the nucleus emits an alpha particle ($\alpha$), which consists of 2 protons and 2 neutrons ($^{4}_{2}\text{He}$).

Applying the laws of conservation of mass and charge:
1. **Mass number ($A$):** Since the alpha particle takes away 4 nucleons, the mass number decreases by 4.
2. **Atomic number ($Z$):** Since the alpha particle takes away 2 protons, the atomic number decreases by 2.

The daughter nucleus after this stage is:

$${}^{A-4}_{Z-2}\text{Y}$$

steps.title: Analyze Beta-Minus Decay
steps.content:
Next, the daughter nucleus undergoes **beta-minus ($\beta^-$) decay**. In this process, a neutron in the nucleus converts into a proton and emits an electron (and an antineutrino).

Applying conservation laws to this stage:
1. **Mass number ($A$):** A neutron is replaced by a proton. Since both have a mass of approximately 1 atomic mass unit, the total number of nucleons remains the same. The mass number is **unchanged**.
2. **Atomic number ($Z$):** Because a new proton is created, the atomic number **increases by 1**.

steps.title: Determine the Final State
steps.content:
Now we combine the changes from both decay stages to find the final configuration relative to the original nucleus ${}^{A}_{Z}\text{X}$:

- **Final Mass Number:** Started at $A$, decreased by 4 in the first step, and remained the same in the second step.
$$A_{final} = A - 4$$

- **Final Atomic Number:** Started at $Z$, decreased by 2 in the first step, and increased by 1 in the second step.
$$Z_{final} = (Z - 2) + 1 = Z - 1$$

So, after both decays, the final nucleus has a mass number of $A-4$ and an atomic number of $Z-1$.

gdc.instructions: Enter -2 + 1 on the main calculation screen.

ti84.instructions: Enter the operation -2 + 1 to find the net change in atomic number.

calculator.description: Verify the simple arithmetic for the atomic number change.

steps.title: Define initial nucleus
steps.content:
To track the changes clearly, let's represent the initial unstable nucleus using standard nuclear notation:

$${}_Z^A X$$

Where:
- $A$ is the **mass number** (total nucleons).
- $Z$ is the **atomic number** (number of protons).

steps.title: Analyze alpha decay
steps.content:
An alpha particle is a helium nucleus, represented as ${}_2^4 \alpha$ or ${}_2^4 \text{He}$. When the nucleus undergoes alpha decay, it ejects 2 protons and 2 neutrons. The nuclear equation is:

$${}_Z^A X \rightarrow {}_{Z-2}^{A-4} Y + {}_2^4 \alpha$$

From this equation, we can see:
- The **mass number decreases by 4** ($A \rightarrow A-4$).
- The **atomic number decreases by 2** ($Z \rightarrow Z-2$).

steps.title: Analyze beta-minus decay
steps.content:
Next, the daughter nucleus (${}_{Z-2}^{A-4} Y$) undergoes beta-minus ($\beta^-$) decay. In this process, a neutron in the nucleus turns into a proton, emitting an electron (${}_{-1}^0 \beta$) and an antineutrino ($\bar{\nu}_e$). The nuclear equation is:

$${}_{Z-2}^{A-4} Y \rightarrow {}_{(Z-2)+1}^{A-4} W + {}_{-1}^0 \beta + \bar{\nu}_e$$

By simplifying the atomic number, $(Z-2) + 1 = Z-1$. Following this decay:
- The **mass number remains unchanged** ($A-4$).
- The **atomic number increases by 1** relative to the daughter nucleus.

steps.title: State final nucleus properties
steps.content:
By combining both steps, we can describe the total change from the original isotope to the final product:

1. **After alpha decay**: Mass number decreases by 4; Atomic number decreases by 2.
2. **After beta-minus decay**: Mass number stays the same; Atomic number increases by 1.
3. **Final Result**: Compared to the original nucleus, the final nucleus has a **mass number of $A-4$** and an **atomic number of $Z-1$**.
