parts.content: Describe how the stability of a nucleus depends on its neutron-to-proton ratio.
parts.markscheme:
- State that for light nuclei (low nucleon number), the neutron-to-proton ratio is approximately 1 ($N \approx Z$) <marks>A1</marks>

- State that for heavier nuclei, the ratio increases to become greater than 1 ($N > Z$) <marks>A1</marks>

- Explain that additional neutrons are required to overcome the electrostatic repulsion between protons <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Analyze the stability trend
steps.content: To describe how stability depends on the neutron-to-proton ratio, we look at how the ratio changes as nuclei get larger. This is a core concept in **Topic E.3: Radioactive Decay**. We will start with light nuclei, then look at heavy nuclei, and finally explain the physics behind the trend.

steps.title: Light nuclei ratio
steps.content:
For light nuclei (those with a low nucleon number, typically up to $Z \approx 20$), stability is found when the number of neutrons ($N$) is approximately equal to the number of protons ($Z$).

In these cases, the ratio is approximately 1:
$$\frac{N}{Z} \approx 1$$

highlights.text: light nuclei (low nucleon number), the neutron-to-proton ratio is approximately 1 ($N \approx Z$)

steps.title: Heavy nuclei ratio
steps.content:
As the nucleus becomes heavier (increasing nucleon number), the stable neutron-to-proton ratio increases to be greater than 1.

For very heavy nuclei, the ratio can reach about 1.5:
$$N > Z$$

highlights.text: heavier nuclei, the ratio increases to become greater than 1 ($N > Z$)

steps.title: Explain the physical cause
steps.content:
The reason for this trend lies in the competition between two fundamental forces:
1. **Strong Nuclear Force:** An attractive force that acts between all nucleons (protons and neutrons) but only over very short distances.
2. **Electrostatic Force:** A repulsive force between protons that acts over longer distances.

As more protons are added, the total **electrostatic repulsion** increases. To keep the nucleus stable, additional neutrons are required to provide more "strong force glue" without adding further electrostatic repulsion.

highlights.text: additional neutrons are required to overcome the electrostatic repulsion between protons

steps.title: Identify opposing nuclear forces
steps.content:
To understand nuclear stability, we must look at the competition between two primary forces acting on nucleons:

1.  **Electrostatic Repulsion**: Protons are positively charged and repel each other. This force is long-range, meaning every proton in the nucleus repels every other proton.
2.  **Strong Nuclear Force (SNF)**: An attractive force that acts between all nucleons (protons and neutrons). While it is much stronger than the electrostatic force, it is very short-range, only acting on immediate neighbors (within $\approx 3 \text{ fm}$).

The neutron-to-proton ratio ($N/Z$) adjusts to balance these forces as the nucleus grows.

steps.title: Stability in light nuclei
steps.content:
For light nuclei (those with a low nucleon number), the stability is achieved when the number of neutrons is approximately equal to the number of protons.

In these small nuclei, the short-range Strong Nuclear Force from a few nucleons is sufficient to overcome the electrostatic repulsion. Therefore, the stable **neutron-to-proton ratio is approximately 1** ($N \approx Z$).

steps.title: Stability in heavy nuclei
steps.content:
As the nucleus gets larger (heavier), the total electrostatic repulsion increases significantly because it is long-range; every new proton added repels all existing protons.

However, because the Strong Nuclear Force is short-range, a proton added to a large nucleus only feels an attraction to its immediate neighbors. To keep the nucleus together, extra "glue" is needed. This glue comes in the form of **additional neutrons**, which contribute to the Strong Nuclear Force without adding any further electrostatic repulsion.

steps.title: State the ratio shift
steps.content:
Because of this need for extra neutrons in larger nuclei, the stable **neutron-to-proton ratio increases to become greater than 1** ($N > Z$).

In summary, for a nucleus to remain stable as it grows:
- Light nuclei: $N \approx Z$
- Heavy nuclei: $N > Z$
- Reason: Extra neutrons provide the Strong Nuclear Force required to overcome the increasing electrostatic repulsion between protons.

highlights.text: stability of a nucleus depends on its neutron-to-proton ratio

parts.content: Explain why unstable nuclei undergo radioactive decay and identify the source of the energy released in this process.
parts.markscheme:
- State that unstable nuclei (with imbalanced neutron-to-proton ratios) decay to achieve a more stable state (higher binding energy per nucleon) <marks>A1</marks>

- Identify that the total mass of the products is less than the mass of the parent nucleus (mass defect) <marks>A1</marks>

- State that this difference in mass is converted into energy according to $E=mc^2$ <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

<note>Award marks for a coherent discussion of stability and energy release.</note>

steps.title: The Drive for Stability
steps.content: Nuclei become unstable when there is an imbalance in their neutron-to-proton ($N:P$) ratio. To correct this instability, these nuclei undergo radioactive decay to transition into a more stable state. This process leads to a nucleus with a higher **binding energy per nucleon**, which represents a more energetically favorable and stable configuration.

steps.title: Identifying the Mass Defect
steps.content:
A key observation in nuclear physics is that the total rest mass of the decay products is always slightly less than the rest mass of the original parent nucleus. This difference in mass is known as the **mass defect**. 

In the context of the IB markscheme, you must explicitly state that the products have less mass than the parent to earn this mark.

steps.title: Mass-Energy Conversion
steps.content:
The "missing mass" (mass defect) is not actually gone; it has been converted into energy. This is governed by Einstein's mass-energy equivalence principle from the Nuclear section of your data booklet:

$$E = mc^2$$

Where:
- $E$ is the energy released
- $m$ is the mass defect (the change in mass)
- $c$ is the speed of light ($3.00 \times 10^8 \text{ m s}^{-1}$)

This released energy typically appears as the kinetic energy of the emitted particles (like alpha or beta particles) or as high-energy photons (gamma radiation).

steps.title: Achieving Nuclear Stability
steps.content: Unstable nuclei typically have an imbalanced **neutron-to-proton ratio**. To reach a more stable state, these nuclei undergo radioactive decay. In nuclear physics, stability is measured by **binding energy per nucleon**. A nucleus will decay if the resulting 'daughter' nucleus (and any emitted particles) has a **higher binding energy per nucleon** than the original 'parent' nucleus. This shift towards a higher binding energy per nucleon represents a move toward a more energetically stable configuration.

steps.title: Identifying the Mass Defect
steps.content: When a nucleus decays into a state with higher binding energy, the nucleons become more tightly bound. Because mass and energy are equivalent, this increase in binding energy results in a **mass defect**. This means the total rest mass of the decay products is slightly **less** than the rest mass of the original parent nucleus.

steps.title: Converting Mass to Energy
steps.content:
The source of the energy released during decay is this difference in mass (the mass defect). According to Einstein’s mass-energy equivalence formula, the 'lost' mass is converted directly into energy:

$$E = mc^{2}$$

This energy is released as the kinetic energy of the emitted particles (like alpha or beta particles) and often as high-energy photons (gamma rays).

parts.content: Calculate the number of $^{210}_{84}\text{Po}$ nuclei in the sample.
parts.markscheme:
Substitute into $n = \frac{m}{M}$: $n = \frac{1.0 \times 10^{-6}}{210} = 4.76 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{mol}$ <marks>M1</marks>
Calculate $N = n \cdot N_A = 4.76 \times 10^{-9} \cdot 6.02 \times 10^{23} \approx 2.87 \times 10^{15}$ nuclei <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Convert mass to grams
steps.content:
To begin, we need to ensure our units are consistent. The molar mass is given in $\text{g mol}^{-1}$, but our sample mass is in micrograms ($\mu\text{g}$). 

We convert the sample mass $m$ into grams:
$$1.0\ \mu\text{g} = 1.0 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{g}$$

steps.title: Calculate the number of moles
steps.content:
Next, we calculate the number of moles ($n$) in the sample. This is a fundamental concept from **Topic B.3: Gas Laws**, where we use the relationship between mass ($m$) and molar mass ($M$):

$$n = \frac{m}{M}$$

Substituting the values:
$$n = \frac{1.0 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{g}}{210\ \text{g mol}^{-1}} \approx 4.76 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{mol}$$

desmos.instructions: Type 1.0 * 10^-6 / 210 in a new expression cell.

calculator.description: Calculate the number of moles by dividing the mass by the molar mass.

steps.title: Determine total nuclei number
steps.content:
Finally, we find the total number of nuclei ($N$) by multiplying the number of moles by Avogadro's constant ($N_A = 6.02 \times 10^{23}\ \text{mol}^{-1}$):

$$N = n \times N_A$$

$$N = (4.7619... \times 10^{-9}\ \text{mol}) \times (6.02 \times 10^{23}\ \text{mol}^{-1})$$
$$N \approx 2.87 \times 10^{15}\ \text{nuclei}$$

desmos.instructions: Type (1.0 * 10^-6 / 210) * 6.02 * 10^23 in a new expression cell.

calculator.description: Multiply the number of moles by Avogadro's constant to find the total nuclei.

steps.title: Define the calculation approach
steps.content:
To find the number of nuclei ($N$) in the sample, we will use the **Unified Atomic Mass Unit** approach. 

This method involves calculating the mass of a single nucleus and then dividing the total mass of the sample by that single-nucleus mass:

$$N = \frac{m_{\text{total}}}{m_{\text{nucleus}}}$$

steps.title: Convert mass to SI units
steps.content:
The question provides the sample mass in micrograms ($\mu\text{g}$). For physics calculations, it is best to convert this to kilograms ($\text{kg}$).

Since $1\ \mu\text{g} = 10^{-6}\ \text{g}$ and $1\ \text{g} = 10^{-3}\ \text{kg}$:

$$1.0\ \mu\text{g} = 1.0 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{kg}$$

steps.title: Mass of one nucleus
steps.content:
The mass of a single nucleus is approximately its mass number ($A$) multiplied by the unified atomic mass unit ($u$). 

For $^{210}_{84}\text{Po}$, the mass number $A = 210$. The unified atomic mass unit is a constant found in the IB Data Booklet:
$$1\ u \approx 1.66 \times 10^{-27}\ \text{kg}$$

So, the mass of one nucleus is:
$$m_{\text{nucleus}} = 210 \times 1.66 \times 10^{-27}\ \text{kg}$$

steps.title: Calculate total number of nuclei
steps.content:
Now, we divide the total sample mass by the mass of a single nucleus:

$$N = \frac{1.0 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{kg}}{210 \times 1.66 \times 10^{-27}\ \text{kg}}$$

$$N \approx 2.87 \times 10^{15}$$

ti84.instructions: Enter the division in the main screen using parentheses for the denominator.

desmos.instructions: Type the expression to find the number of nuclei.

calculator.description: Calculate the total number of nuclei by dividing the sample mass by the mass of one nucleus.

steps.title: Final Answer
steps.content:
The number of $^{210}_{84}\text{Po}$ nuclei in the sample is $2.87 \times 10^{15}$.

This matches the result expected by the markscheme using the molar mass method ($2.87 \times 10^{15}$ nuclei), confirming our approach is correct.

parts.content: Determine the decay constant of $^{210}_{84}\text{Po}$ in $\text{s}^{-1}$.
parts.markscheme:
Convert half-life to seconds: $T_{1/2} = 138.4 \times 24 \times 3600 = 1.20 \times 10^7\ \text{s}$ <marks>M1</marks>
Calculate $\lambda = \frac{\ln 2}{T_{1/2}} = \frac{0.693}{1.196 \times 10^7} \approx 5.80 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{s}^{-1}$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Convert half-life to seconds
steps.content:
To find the decay constant in $\text{s}^{-1}$, we first need to convert the given half-life from days into seconds. 

There are $24$ hours in a day, $60$ minutes in an hour, and $60$ seconds in a minute. Therefore, there are $86,400$ seconds in one day ($24 \times 3600$).

$$\begin{aligned} T_{1/2} &= 138.4 \times 24 \times 3600 \\ T_{1/2} &= 11,957,760 \text{ s} \end{aligned}$$

In scientific notation, this is approximately $1.20 \times 10^7 \text{ s}$. Converting this value correctly earns the first mark.

calculator.description: Calculate the total number of seconds in 138.4 days.

steps.title: Calculate the decay constant
steps.content:
Now we use the standard relationship between the decay constant ($\lambda$) and the half-life ($T_{1/2}$), which you can find in **Topic E.3: Radioactive decay**:

$$\lambda = \frac{\ln 2}{T_{1/2}}$$

Substituting our value in seconds:

$$\begin{aligned} \lambda &= \frac{\ln 2}{11,957,760} \\ \lambda &\approx 5.80 \times 10^{-8} \text{ s}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

Rounding to three significant figures gives the final result required by the markscheme.

gdc.instructions: Use the ln function, enter 2, and divide by 11957760.

ti84.instructions: Press the [ln] key, enter 2, close parentheses, then divide by 11957760 and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Type ln(2) / 11957760 into the input bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the decay constant using the natural log of 2.

steps.title: Identify given half-life
steps.content:
From the question, we are given the half-life ($T_{1/2}$) of $^{210}_{84}\text{Po}$ in days. This is our starting point for finding the decay constant.

$$T_{1/2} = 138.4\ \text{days}$$

steps.title: Calculate decay constant (days⁻¹)
steps.content:
The decay constant ($\lambda$) is related to the half-life by the formula found in **Topic E.3 Radioactive Decay**:

$$\lambda = \frac{\ln 2}{T_{1/2}}$$

Substituting our value in days:

$$\lambda = \frac{\ln 2}{138.4} \approx 0.005008\ \text{days}^{-1}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the decay constant in reciprocal days.

steps.title: Calculate seconds per day
steps.content:
To convert the unit from per day ($\text{days}^{-1}$) to per second ($\text{s}^{-1}$), we first need to know how many seconds are in one day:

$$\text{Seconds in a day} = 24\ \text{hours} \times 60\ \text{minutes} \times 60\ \text{seconds}$$
$$\text{Seconds in a day} = 86,400\ \text{s}$$

calculator.description: Find total seconds in one day.

steps.title: Convert to per second
steps.content:
Because the unit is $\text{days}^{-1}$ (reciprocal days), we must divide our previous result by the number of seconds in a day to get $\text{s}^{-1}$:

$$\lambda_{\text{s}^{-1}} = \frac{\lambda_{\text{days}^{-1}}}{86,400}$$

$$\lambda = \frac{0.005008...}{86,400} \approx 5.80 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{s}^{-1}$$

calculator.description: Divide the decay constant in days by the seconds in a day.

steps.title: Final Result
steps.content:
Rounding to three significant figures (to match the precision of $138.4$), we get:

$$\lambda = 5.80 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{s}^{-1}$$

$A = 1.66 \times 10^8\ \text{Bq}$ <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Find number of nuclei
steps.content:
To find the number of nuclei ($N$), we first determine how many moles ($n$) are in the $1.0\ \mu\text{g}$ sample and then multiply by Avogadro's constant ($N_A = 6.02 \times 10^{23}\ \text{mol}^{-1}$).

Convert mass to grams: $1.0\ \mu\text{g} = 1.0 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{g}$.

Using the molar mass $M = 210\ \text{g\ mol}^{-1}$:

$$n = \frac{m}{M} = \frac{1.0 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{g}}{210\ \text{g\ mol}^{-1}} \approx 4.76 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{mol}$$

$$N = n \times N_A = (4.76 \times 10^{-9}) \times (6.02 \times 10^{23}) \approx 2.87 \times 10^{15}\ \text{nuclei}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the total number of nuclei in the sample.

steps.title: Calculate decay constant
steps.content:
Next, we calculate the decay constant ($\lambda$). The activity formula requires the decay constant in reciprocal seconds ($\text{s}^{-1}$), so we must convert the half-life ($T_{1/2}$) into seconds.

$$T_{1/2} = 138.4\ \text{days} \times 24\ \text{h/day} \times 3600\ \text{s/h} = 11,957,760\ \text{s}$$

Now apply the formula from **Topic E.3 Radioactive decay**:

$$\lambda = \frac{\ln(2)}{T_{1/2}} = \frac{0.693}{11,957,760} \approx 5.80 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{s}^{-1}$$

calculator.description: Convert half-life to seconds and find the decay constant.

steps.title: Determine initial activity
steps.content:
Finally, we find the initial activity ($A$) using the relationship from **Topic E.3 Radioactive decay**:

$$A = \lambda N$$

Substitute the values we calculated:

$$A = (5.80 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{s}^{-1}) \times (2.87 \times 10^{15})$$

$$A = 1.66 \times 10^8\ \text{Bq}$$

This value represents the number of alpha decays occurring per second at the start of the study.

calculator.description: Multiply the decay constant by the number of nuclei to find the activity.

steps.title: Calculate the decay constant
steps.content:
To find the activity, we first need the decay constant $\lambda$. This is calculated from the half-life $T_{1/2}$, but we must convert the time into seconds to get the activity in Becquerels (decays per second).

$$\begin{aligned} T_{1/2} &= 138.4 \times 24 \times 3600 \text{ s} \\ T_{1/2} &= 11,957,760 \text{ s} \end{aligned}$$

Using the formula $\lambda = \frac{\ln 2}{T_{1/2}}$:

$$\begin{aligned} \lambda &= \frac{\ln 2}{11,957,760} \\ \lambda &\approx 5.80 \times 10^{-8} \text{ s}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

This formula is found in **Topic E.3: Radioactive decay** of your IB Physics syllabus.

calculator.description: Convert half-life to seconds and find the decay constant.

steps.title: Estimate mass of one nucleus
steps.content:
Using the **Atomic Mass Unit Approach**, we estimate the mass of a single $^{210}\text{Po}$ nucleus. The mass number is $210$, meaning there are $210$ nucleons. We multiply this by the unified atomic mass unit $u \approx 1.661 \times 10^{-27} \text{ kg}$.

$$\begin{aligned} m_{\text{nucleus}} &= 210 \times u \\ m_{\text{nucleus}} &= 210 \times 1.661 \times 10^{-27} \text{ kg} \\ m_{\text{nucleus}} &\approx 3.488 \times 10^{-25} \text{ kg} \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the approximate mass of one Po-210 atom in kg.

steps.title: Find total number of nuclei
steps.content:
Now, we find the total number of nuclei $N$ by dividing the total mass of the sample by the mass of a single nucleus. First, convert the sample mass from micrograms to kilograms:

$$1.0 \text{ } \mu\text{g} = 1.0 \times 10^{-9} \text{ kg}$$

Now calculate $N$:

$$\begin{aligned} N &= \frac{m_{\text{sample}}}{m_{\text{nucleus}}} \\ N &= \frac{1.0 \times 10^{-9}}{3.488 \times 10^{-25}} \\ N &\approx 2.87 \times 10^{15} \text{ nuclei} \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Divide total mass by single atom mass to find number of nuclei.

steps.title: Calculate initial activity
steps.content:
Finally, we calculate the activity $A$ using the relationship between activity, the decay constant, and the number of nuclei.

$$\begin{aligned} A &= \lambda N \\ A &= (5.80 \times 10^{-8}) \times (2.87 \times 10^{15}) \\ A &\approx 1.66 \times 10^{8} \text{ Bq} \end{aligned}$$

This matches the markscheme's value of $1.66 \times 10^8 \text{ Bq}$, earning you the final 2 marks.

calculator.description: Multiply the decay constant by the number of nuclei to find the activity.

parts.content: Determine the energy released per second as alpha particle kinetic energy, given each alpha particle carries $5.30\ \text{MeV}$.
parts.markscheme:
Recognize that power $P = A \cdot E$ and convert $\text{MeV}$ to $\text{J}$: $P = 1.66 \times 10^8 \cdot 5.30 \times 10^6 \cdot 1.60 \times 10^{-19}$ <marks>M1</marks>
$P = 1.41 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{W}$ (or $0.141\ \text{mW}$) <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Calculate number of nuclei
steps.content:
To find the energy released per second, we first need to determine how many nuclei of $^{210}\text{Po}$ are in our $1.0\ \mu\text{g}$ sample. 

First, convert the mass to grams: $1.0\ \mu\text{g} = 1.0 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{g}$.

Using the molar mass ($M = 210\ \text{g\ mol}^{-1}$) and Avogadro's constant ($N_A \approx 6.02 \times 10^{23}\ \text{mol}^{-1}$), we calculate the number of nuclei $N$:

$$N = \frac{m}{M} \times N_A = \frac{1.0 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{g}}{210\ \text{g\ mol}^{-1}} \times 6.02 \times 10^{23}\ \text{mol}^{-1} \approx 2.87 \times 10^{15}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the total number of nuclei N.

steps.title: Calculate the activity
steps.content:
The activity $A$ is the number of decays per second, given by $A = \lambda N$. We first find the decay constant $\lambda$ from the half-life ($T_{1/2}$), ensuring the time is in seconds.

$$T_{1/2} = 138.4 \times 24 \times 3600\ \text{s} \approx 1.20 \times 10^7\ \text{s}$$

$$\lambda = \frac{\ln(2)}{T_{1/2}} \approx 5.80 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{s}^{-1}$$

Now, calculate the activity:

$$A = \lambda N = (5.80 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{s}^{-1}) \times (2.87 \times 10^{15}) \approx 1.66 \times 10^8\ \text{Bq}$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate lambda: ln(2) / (138.4 * 24 * 3600). Multiply by N: ans * 2.87E15

ti84.instructions: Calculate lambda: ln(2) / (138.4 * 24 * 3600). Multiply by N: ans * 2.87E15

calculator.description: Calculate the activity A in Becquerels (decays per second).

steps.title: Determine total energy per second
steps.content:
Energy per second is power $P$. Since each decay releases one alpha particle with energy $E_\alpha$, the total power is:

$$P = A \times E_\alpha$$

Convert $5.30\ \text{MeV}$ to Joules using the conversion factor $1.60 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{J/eV}$ from the data booklet:

$$E_\alpha = 5.30 \times 10^6 \times 1.60 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{J} = 8.48 \times 10^{-13}\ \text{J}$$

Finally, multiply the activity by the energy per particle:

$$P = (1.66 \times 10^8\ \text{s}^{-1}) \times (8.48 \times 10^{-13}\ \text{J}) \approx 1.41 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{W}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the final power output in Watts.

steps.title: State final answer
steps.content:
The energy released per second as alpha particle kinetic energy is $1.41 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{W}$ (or $0.141\ \text{mW}$).

You earn **1 mark** for correctly calculating the activity or setting up the power equation with appropriate unit conversions, and **1 mark** for the correct final answer.

parts.content: Discuss why alpha-emitting isotopes like $^{210}_{84}\text{Po}$ can be extremely dangerous despite having low penetration power.
parts.markscheme:
State that alpha particles have a high ionization density/power, depositing all energy locally <marks>R1</marks>
Explain that if ingested or inhaled, they cause severe biological damage/cell mutations to internal tissues due to lack of shielding <marks>R1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: High ionization density
steps.content: Alpha particles are relatively large, heavy particles (helium nuclei) with a $+2$ charge. Because of this, they are **highly ionizing**, meaning they frequently interact with and strip electrons from atoms they encounter. This results in a **high ionization density**, where the particle deposits all of its kinetic energy over a very short distance (locally) rather than spreading it out.

highlights.text: alpha particles have a high ionization density/power, depositing all energy locally

steps.title: Internal exposure hazards
steps.content:
Although alpha particles are easily stopped by a sheet of paper or the dead layer of human skin, they become extremely dangerous if the isotope is **ingested or inhaled**. 

Inside the body, there is no protective barrier (like skin) to shield sensitive internal organs. The alpha particles come into direct contact with living cells, where their high ionizing power causes severe **biological damage**, such as DNA mutations or cell death, leading to serious health risks like cancer.

highlights.text: if ingested or inhaled, they cause severe biological damage/cell mutations to internal tissues due to lack of shielding

Over a period of 12 days, the researcher measures the count rate at regular intervals and notes that the data fits an exponential decay model. The isotope has a half-life of 4.0 days.

parts.content: Calculate the percentage of the original nuclei that remain undecayed after 12 days.
parts.markscheme:
- Use of decay formula with $n = \frac{12}{4} = 3$: $N = N_0 \left( \frac{1}{2} \right)^3$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Percentage remaining $= 12.5\%$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

parts.content: Determine the number of half-lives that have elapsed.
parts.markscheme:
- $n = \frac{12}{4} = 3$ <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Identify the time parameters
steps.content:
To find how many half-lives have passed, we first need to identify two key values from the question: the total time the gas has been decaying and the duration of a single half-life.

- Total time elapsed ($t$): $12$ days
- Half-life ($T_{1/2}$): $4.0$ days

steps.title: Calculate the half-life ratio
steps.content:
The number of half-lives, $n$, is the ratio of the total time elapsed to the time it takes for one half-life to occur. We can calculate this using the following relationship:

$$n = \frac{\text{Total time elapsed}}{\text{Half-life}}$$

Substituting our values:

$$n = \frac{12}{4.0}$$

gdc.instructions: In the Run-Matrix menu, enter 12 / 4.0 and press EXE.

ti84.instructions: Enter 12 / 4.0 on the home screen and press ENTER.

calculator.description: Calculate the number of half-lives by dividing the total time by the half-life duration.

steps.title: State the final result
steps.content:
Performing the division gives us the total count of half-lives that have elapsed during the study.

$$n = 3$$

According to the markscheme, showing this division earns the **A1** mark.

steps.title: Identify given time values
steps.content:
To determine how many half-lives have passed, we first identify the total duration of the observation and the length of a single half-life from the question.

- Total time ($t$) = $12$ days
- Half-life ($T_{1/2}$) = $4.0$ days

This concept is part of **Topic E.3: Radioactive decay**.

steps.title: Count the half-life intervals
steps.content:
Using the iterative counting approach, we step through time in increments of the half-life until we reach the total time of $12$ days:

1. After **one** half-life: $4.0$ days have passed.
2. After **two** half-lives: $4.0 + 4.0 = 8.0$ days have passed.
3. After **three** half-lives: $8.0 + 4.0 = 12.0$ days have passed.

We have reached the total time of $12$ days.

gdc.instructions: Multiply 3 by 4 to confirm it equals 12.

ti84.instructions: Multiply 3 by 4 to confirm it equals 12.

desmos.instructions: Multiply 3 by 4 to confirm it equals 12.

calculator.description: Verify the total time reached after 3 intervals of 4 days.

steps.title: State the final number
steps.content:
Since it took three intervals of $4.0$ days to reach the total time of $12$ days, the number of half-lives ($n$) is $3$.

$$n = \frac{12}{4} = 3$$

parts.content: Calculate the time it takes for the activity to fall to 5% of its original value.
parts.markscheme:
- Calculate $\lambda$ and substitute into decay equation: $\lambda = \frac{\ln 2}{4} \approx 0.1733\ \text{day}^{-1}$ <marks>M1</marks>
- $t = \frac{\ln(0.05)}{-0.1733} \approx 17.3$ days <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify given values
steps.content:
To find the time required for the activity to drop to $5\%$, we first identify the known variables from the question:
- Half-life ($T_{1/2}$) = $4.0$ days
- Final activity ($A$) = $5\%$ of original activity ($A_0$), which can be written as $A = 0.05 A_0$.

This problem uses concepts from **Topic E.3: Radioactive decay**.

steps.title: Calculate the decay constant
steps.content:
The decay constant ($\lambda$) represents the probability of decay per unit time. We calculate it using the relationship between half-life and the natural log of 2:

$$\lambda = \frac{\ln 2}{T_{1/2}}$$

Substituting our value:

$$\lambda = \frac{\ln 2}{4.0} \approx 0.1733 \text{ day}^{-1}$$

gdc.instructions: Type ln(2)/4 into the calculation screen.

desmos.instructions: Type ln(2)/4 into a calculation cell.

calculator.description: Calculate the decay constant λ.

steps.title: Set up the decay equation
steps.content:
The activity of a radioactive sample follows the exponential decay law:

$$A = A_0 e^{-\lambda t}$$

Since we want to find the time $t$ when $A = 0.05 A_0$, we substitute this into the equation:

$$0.05 A_0 = A_0 e^{-\lambda t}$$

Dividing both sides by $A_0$ gives us the ratio:

$$0.05 = e^{-\lambda t}$$

steps.title: Solve for time (t)
steps.content:
To solve for $t$, we take the natural logarithm ($\ln$) of both sides to cancel out the exponential ($e$):

$$\ln(0.05) = -\lambda t$$

Now, rearrange for $t$:

$$t = \frac{\ln(0.05)}{-\lambda}$$

Substitute $\lambda \approx 0.1733$:

$$t = \frac{\ln(0.05)}{-0.1733} \approx 17.3 \text{ days}$$

In an IB exam, showing the calculation for $\lambda$ and the substitution into the decay equation earns the first mark ($M1$), and the correct final answer with units earns the second ($A1$).

gdc.instructions: Type ln(0.05)/-0.1733 into the calculation screen.

ti84.instructions: Press [ln], [0.05], [)], [/], [(-)], then use the previous answer [2nd] [ANS] or type [0.1733], then [ENTER].

desmos.instructions: Type ln(0.05)/-0.1733 into a calculation cell.

calculator.description: Calculate the time t.

steps.title: Identify the decay relationship
steps.content:
To find the time taken for a radioactive substance to decay to a certain level, we can use the **Half-life Power Method**. This relates the remaining activity ($A$) to the original activity ($A_0$) based on the number of half-lives that have passed:

$$A = A_0 \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{\frac{t}{T_{1/2}}}$$

Where:
- $A$ is the final activity
- $A_0$ is the initial activity
- $t$ is the time elapsed
- $T_{1/2}$ is the half-life

steps.title: Substitute known values
steps.content:
The question states the activity falls to **5% of its original value**. This means the ratio $\frac{A}{A_0} = 0.05$. We also know the **half-life is 4.0 days**.

Substituting these into our equation:

$$0.05 = (0.5)^{\frac{t}{4.0}}$$

steps.title: Solve using logarithms
steps.content:
To solve for the exponent $t$, we take the natural logarithm ($\ln$) of both sides of the equation:

$$\ln(0.05) = \ln\left(0.5^{\frac{t}{4.0}}\right)$$

Using the logarithm power rule, $\ln(x^n) = n\ln(x)$:

$$\ln(0.05) = \frac{t}{4.0} \ln(0.5)$$

Now, rearrange for $t$:

$$t = \frac{4.0 \times \ln(0.05)}{\ln(0.5)}$$

steps.title: Calculate the final time
steps.content:
Using a calculator to find the final value:

$$\begin{aligned} t &= \frac{4.0 \times (-2.9957...)}{-0.6931...} \\ t &\approx 17.3 \text{ days} \end{aligned}$$

This makes sense physically: after 4 half-lives (16 days), the activity would be $0.5^4 = 6.25\%$. Since we want to reach 5%, the time should be slightly longer than 16 days.

gdc.instructions: Use the log or ln keys to evaluate the expression.

ti84.instructions: Enter the expression into the main screen.

desmos.instructions: Type the formula directly into a cell.

calculator.description: Calculate the time using the logarithmic expression.

parts.content: Explain why an exponential model is appropriate for radioactive decay and how the researcher can use it to estimate the half-life.
parts.markscheme:
- States that radioactive decay is a random process (over large samples) <marks>A1</marks>
- Describes plotting $\ln(\text{count rate})$ vs time to obtain a straight line where gradient is $-\lambda$ (or similar valid method) <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Understand the random nature
steps.content:
Radioactive decay is fundamentally a **random process** at the level of individual nuclei. While we cannot predict exactly when a single nucleus will decay, for a large sample of nuclei, the rate of decay is proportional to the number of nuclei present. 

This statistical behavior is what gives rise to the exponential decay model, as the probability of any given nucleus decaying in a specific time interval remains constant.

highlights.text: radioactive decay is a random process

steps.title: Apply logarithmic linearization
steps.content:
The exponential decay of the count rate $C$ over time $t$ is expressed as:

$$C = C_0 e^{-\lambda t}$$

Where $C_0$ is the initial count rate and $\lambda$ is the decay constant. To turn this into a linear relationship that's easier to analyze, we take the natural logarithm ($
\ln$) of both sides:

$$\begin{aligned} \ln(C) &= \ln(C_0 e^{-\lambda t}) \\ \ln(C) &= \ln(C_0) + \ln(e^{-\lambda t}) \\ \ln(C) &= -\lambda t + \ln(C_0) \end{aligned}$$

This matches the equation for a straight line, $y = mx + c$, where $y = \ln(C)$, $x = t$, and the gradient $m = -\lambda$.

steps.title: Extract the half-life
steps.content:
The researcher can plot a graph of **$\ln(\text{count rate})$ vs time**. By calculating the **gradient** of the resulting straight line, they find the value of $-\lambda$.

Once the decay constant $\lambda$ is determined, the half-life $T_{1/2}$ can be calculated using the following relationship found in **Topic E.3 Radioactive decay**:

$$T_{1/2} = \frac{\ln(2)}{\lambda}$$

This linearization method is more accurate than reading directly from a curve because it utilizes all data points to find a line of best fit.

gdc.instructions:
1. Enter time and count rate into lists. 
2. Create a new list for the natural log of the count rate. 
3. Perform a linear regression (y = ax + b) on Time vs ln(Count Rate). 
4. The slope 'a' corresponds to -λ.

ti84.instructions:
1. Enter time in L1 and Count Rate in L2. 
2. In L3, define it as ln(L2). 
3. Go to [STAT] -> [CALC] -> [4:LinReg(ax+b)]. 
4. Use L1 for Xlist and L3 for Ylist. 
5. The 'a' value (gradient) is -λ.

desmos.instructions:
1. Create a table with time (x1) and count rate (y1). 
2. Add a new column for ln(y1). 
3. Use the regression model: ln(y1) ~ m*x1 + b. 
4. The value of m is your -λ.

calculator.description: If you have a set of count rate and time data, you can use a graphing calculator to find the decay constant quickly using linear regression on the logged data.

steps.title: Explain the Random Nature
steps.content:
Radioactive decay is a **random process** at the level of individual atoms. While we cannot predict exactly when a specific nucleus will decay, for a large sample of nuclei, the rate of decay (the count rate) is directly proportional to the number of radioactive nuclei present.

This constant probability of decay per unit time leads naturally to an **exponential decay model**, where the quantity decreases at a rate proportional to its current value.

steps.title: Plotting the Data
steps.content:
Using the **Direct Graphical Analysis** method, the researcher should plot the measured **count rate** on the $y$-axis and **time** on the $x$-axis. 

Because the decay is exponential, the resulting graph will be a smooth curve that starts high and levels off toward zero, known as a decay curve.

steps.title: Estimating Half-life Graphically
steps.content:
To estimate the half-life ($T_{1/2}$) from this graph, follow these steps:

1. Identify an initial count rate ($A_0$) at a specific time ($t_1$).
2. Determine the time ($t_2$) when the count rate has dropped to exactly half of that value ($A_0 / 2$).
3. The difference in time, $\Delta t = t_2 - t_1$, is the **half-life**.

To improve accuracy, the researcher can repeat this process for multiple points on the curve (e.g., from $A_0/2$ to $A_0/4$) and calculate the average time interval.

gdc.instructions:
1. In the Graphing app, enter f1(x) = 100 * (0.5)^(x/4).
2. Enter f2(x) = 50.
3. Use the 'Intersection' tool (Analyze Graph > Intersection) to find where the count rate is halved.
4. Observe that the x-value of the intersection point represents the half-life.

ti84.instructions:
1. Press [Y=] and enter the decay equation: Y1 = 100 * (0.5)^(X/4) (using the 4-day half-life as an example).
2. Enter Y2 = 50 (half of the initial 100).
3. Press [GRAPH] and adjust [WINDOW] (Xmax=12, Ymax=110).
4. Press [2nd] [CALC] and select '5: intersect'.
5. Select both curves and press [ENTER].

desmos.instructions:
1. Type y = 100 * (0.5)^(x/4) into the first expression line.
2. Type y = 50 into the second expression line.
3. Click on the point where the two lines intersect.
4. The x-coordinate of the intersection (4) is the half-life.

calculator.description: Visualizing exponential decay and finding the half-life point.

steps.title: Final Answer Summary
steps.content: In summary, the exponential model is appropriate because radioactive decay is a **random process**. The researcher estimates the half-life by plotting **count rate against time** and measuring the **time interval** required for the count rate to decrease by **half** of its initial value.

parts.content: Suggest a method to distinguish between the beta and gamma emissions in this experiment.
parts.markscheme:
- Use different absorbers (e.g., aluminium to block beta, lead to block gamma) to determine which radiation is present <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Identify radiation properties
steps.content:
To distinguish between radiation types, we use the fact that they have different penetrating powers. This is a core concept from **Topic E.3: Radioactive decay**. 

*   **Beta radiation** ($\beta$) is moderately penetrating and can be stopped by a thin sheet of metal.
*   **Gamma radiation** ($\gamma$) is highly penetrating and requires dense materials to be significantly reduced.

highlights.text: Suggest a method to distinguish between the beta and gamma emissions

steps.title: Use an aluminium absorber
steps.content:
Place a few millimeters of **aluminium** between the radioactive source and the detector. 

Since aluminium stops beta particles but allows most gamma rays to pass through, any remaining count rate measured by the detector will primarily be due to the gamma radiation.

steps.title: Use a lead absorber
steps.content:
Next, replace the aluminium with several centimeters of **lead**. 

Lead is dense enough to significantly attenuate (absorb) gamma radiation. If the count rate drops significantly more than it did with the aluminium, it confirms the presence of gamma radiation.

steps.title: State the conclusion
steps.content:
By comparing the count rates with no absorber, an aluminium absorber, and a lead absorber, you can determine the proportion of the total activity that comes from each type of radiation. 

In an exam, simply stating that you would use **different absorbers** (like aluminium for beta and lead for gamma) is enough to earn the mark.

highlights.text: Use different absorbers (e.g., aluminium to block beta, lead to block gamma) to determine which radiation is present

steps.title: Identify the physical properties
steps.content: To distinguish between beta ($\beta$) and gamma ($\gamma$) radiation, we first look at their physical properties. Beta particles are charged (they are either electrons or positrons), whereas gamma rays are neutral photons with no charge. This difference is key to using a magnetic field for separation.

steps.title: Apply a magnetic field
steps.content: When a charged particle moves through a magnetic field, it experiences a magnetic force. We can pass the mixed radiation beam through a region with a strong, uniform magnetic field. This concept is covered in **Topic D.3: Motion in electromagnetic fields**.

steps.title: Use the force equation
steps.content:
According to the IB Physics data booklet, the force $F$ on a particle with charge $q$ moving with velocity $v$ in a magnetic field $B$ is:

$$F = qvB \sin\theta$$

Since gamma rays have no charge ($q = 0$), the force acting on them is zero ($F = 0$). However, because beta particles have a charge ($q = \pm e$), they will experience a non-zero force.

steps.title: Observe the deflection
steps.content:
As the radiation passes through the field:
1. **Beta particles** will follow a curved path (deflect) because of the magnetic force.
2. **Gamma rays** will continue in a straight line (remain undeflected) because they are neutral.

By placing a detector behind the magnetic field, you can distinguish them by seeing which part of the signal stayed centered and which part was shifted to the side.

parts.content: Write an equation for a $\beta^-$ decay reaction of the isotope ${}_{15}^{32}\mathrm{P}$.
parts.markscheme:
- State the decay equation: ${}_{15}^{32}\mathrm{P} \to {}_{16}^{32}\mathrm{S} + \beta^- + \bar{\nu}_e$ <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

methods.label: Conservation of Mass and Proton Numbers

steps.title: Identify the decay process
steps.content:
The question asks for the $\beta^-$ decay of ${}_{15}^{32}\mathrm{P}$. In $\beta^-$ decay, a neutron in the nucleus transforms into a proton, emitting an electron (the beta particle, ${}_{-1}^{0}\beta$ or $\beta^-$) and an electron antineutrino ($\bar{\nu}_e$). 

This process is covered in **Topic E.3: Radioactive decay**. To find the resulting daughter nucleus, we must ensure that both the total mass number and the total proton number are conserved.

steps.title: Conserve the mass number
steps.content:
The mass number ($A$) is the top number in the nuclear notation. For the conservation of mass number, the sum of $A$ on the left side of the equation must equal the sum of $A$ on the right side.

$$\begin{aligned} A_{\text{parent}} &= A_{\text{daughter}} + A_{\text{beta}} + A_{\text{antineutrino}} \\ 32 &= A_{\text{daughter}} + 0 + 0 \\ A_{\text{daughter}} &= 32 \end{aligned}$$

The mass number of the daughter nucleus remains **32**.

steps.title: Conserve the proton number
steps.content:
The proton number ($Z$) is the bottom number, representing the charge. We must balance the total charge on both sides of the equation. Note that a $\beta^-$ particle has a charge of $-1$.

$$\begin{aligned} Z_{\text{parent}} &= Z_{\text{daughter}} + Z_{\text{beta}} + Z_{\text{antineutrino}} \\ 15 &= Z_{\text{daughter}} + (-1) + 0 \\ 15 &= Z_{\text{daughter}} - 1 \\ Z_{\text{daughter}} &= 16 \end{aligned}$$

The atomic number of the daughter nucleus is **16**.

steps.title: Identify the daughter element
steps.content:
Using the periodic table (or general chemical knowledge required for IB Physics), we identify the element with atomic number $Z = 16$. 

Element 16 is **Sulfur (S)**. Therefore, the daughter nucleus is ${}_{16}^{32}\mathrm{S}$.

steps.title: Write the final equation
steps.content:
Now we combine all components into a single nuclear equation. Remember to include the electron antineutrino ($\bar{\nu}_e$) to satisfy the conservation of lepton number, which is a requirement for a complete decay equation.

$${}_{15}^{32}\mathrm{P} \to {}_{16}^{32}\mathrm{S} + \beta^- + \bar{\nu}_e$$

steps.title: Identify the parent nucleus
steps.content:
To write the decay equation, we first identify the parent nucleus provided in the question. We are starting with Phosphorus-32:

$${}_{15}^{32}\mathrm{P}$$

Here, the mass number ($A$) is 32 and the atomic number ($Z$) is 15. This topic is part of **Topic E.3: Radioactive decay** in your syllabus.

steps.title: Apply beta-minus decay pattern
steps.content:
In $\beta^-$ decay, a neutron in the nucleus transforms into a proton. This follows a specific pattern:
1. The atomic number increases by 1 ($Z \to Z+1$).
2. The mass number remains the same ($A \to A$).

Calculating the new atomic number:
$$Z_{new} = 15 + 1 = 16$$

Looking at the periodic table, the element with atomic number 16 is Sulfur (S). So, our daughter nucleus is:

$${}_{16}^{32}\mathrm{S}$$

gdc.instructions: Perform basic addition to find the new atomic number.

ti84.instructions: Simply add 1 to the original atomic number.

desmos.instructions: Add 1 to the starting atomic number of 15.

calculator.description: Calculate the new atomic number for the daughter nucleus.

steps.title: Include the emitted particles
steps.content:
According to the standard $\beta^-$ decay pattern, the transformation also releases two extra particles to conserve charge and lepton number:
- A $\beta^-$ particle (an electron).
- An electron antineutrino ($\bar{\nu}_e$).

steps.title: Construct the final equation
steps.content:
Now we combine all the components into a single balanced nuclear equation. 

$${}_{15}^{32}\mathrm{P} \to {}_{16}^{32}\mathrm{S} + \beta^- + \bar{\nu}_e$$

To ensure full marks, always remember to include the antineutrino ($\bar{\nu}_e$), as it is required for the conservation of lepton number.

parts.content: Determine the daughter nucleus produced in this reaction.
parts.markscheme:
- State the daughter nucleus: ${}_{16}^{32}\mathrm{S}$ <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Identify the decay type
steps.content:
In this problem, we are looking at $\beta^-$ (beta-minus) decay. In $\beta^-$ decay, a neutron in the parent nucleus transforms into a proton, and an electron (the beta particle) and an electron antineutrino are emitted. 

This process is covered in **Topic E.3: Radioactive decay**. Based on the common isotope studies in this context (and the markscheme), we are starting with Phosphorus-32 (${}_{15}^{32}\mathrm{P}$).

steps.title: State conservation laws
steps.content:
To find the daughter nucleus, we must balance the nuclear equation. There are two main conservation laws to apply:
1. **Conservation of Mass Number ($A$):** The total number of nucleons (protons + neutrons) must be the same on both sides.
2. **Conservation of Atomic Number ($Z$):** The total charge must be the same on both sides.

steps.title: Set up the equation
steps.content:
Let's represent the daughter nucleus as ${}_{Z}^{A}\mathrm{Y}$. The $\beta^-$ particle is an electron, represented as ${}_{-1}^{0}\mathrm{e}$, and we also have an antineutrino ${}_{0}^{0}\bar{\nu}_e$. The general equation is:

$${}_{15}^{32}\mathrm{P} \rightarrow {}_{Z}^{A}\mathrm{Y} + {}_{-1}^{0}\mathrm{e} + {}_{0}^{0}\bar{\nu}_e$$

steps.title: Solve for A and Z
steps.content:
Now we balance the top (mass) and bottom (atomic) numbers:

**For Mass Number ($A$):**
$$32 = A + 0 + 0 \implies A = 32$$

**For Atomic Number ($Z$):**
$$15 = Z + (-1) + 0$$

ti84.instructions: Add 1 to both sides of the equation: 15 + 1

calculator.description: Calculate the atomic number Z by rearranging the equation.

steps.title: Identify the daughter nucleus
steps.content:
From our calculation, $Z = 16$. Using the periodic table, the element with atomic number 16 is Sulfur (S). 

Combining the mass number $A=32$ and atomic number $Z=16$, the daughter nucleus is:

$${}_{16}^{32}\mathrm{S}$$

steps.title: Recall beta-minus decay rules
steps.content:
In $\beta^-$ decay, a neutron in the nucleus is transformed into a proton, an electron (the beta particle), and an antineutrino. This process is covered in **Topic E.3: Radioactive decay**.

According to the rules for $\beta^-$ decay:
1. The **mass number ($A$)** remains unchanged because the total number of nucleons (protons + neutrons) stays the same.
2. The **atomic number ($Z$)** increases by 1 because a neutron has turned into a proton.

steps.title: Apply rules to Phosphorus-32
steps.content:
From the context of this study (specifically the decay of Phosphorus-32, which is the parent isotope ${}_{15}^{32}\mathrm{P}$), we apply the rules to find the new mass and atomic numbers:

$$\begin{aligned} A_{daughter} &= A_{parent} = 32 \\ Z_{daughter} &= Z_{parent} + 1 = 15 + 1 \end{aligned}$$

Let's calculate the new atomic number.

ti84.instructions: Add 1 to the original atomic number of 15.

desmos.instructions: Enter 15 + 1 to find the resulting atomic number.

calculator.description: Calculate the atomic number of the daughter nucleus.

steps.title: Identify the daughter nucleus
steps.content:
The new atomic number is $Z = 16$. By looking at the periodic table, we can identify that the element with atomic number 16 is **Sulfur (S)**.

Combining the mass number ($A=32$) and the atomic number ($Z=16$), we write the daughter nucleus in standard notation:

$${}_{16}^{32}\mathrm{S}$$

2 marks total

parts.content: Calculate the time required for a sample of ${}_{15}^{32}\mathrm{P}$ with half-life 14.3 days to decay to 10% of its original number of nuclei.
parts.markscheme:
- Calculate the decay constant using $\lambda = \frac{\ln 2}{T_{1/2}}$: $\lambda = \frac{0.693}{14.3} \approx 0.0485 \text{ day}^{-1}$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Substitute into $N = N_0 e^{-\lambda t}$ and solve for $t$: $0.10 = e^{-0.0485t} \Rightarrow t = \frac{\ln(0.10)}{-0.0485} \approx 47.5$ days <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

parts.content: Explain why the mass of the product nucleus is less than the original mass, even though only an electron and antineutrino are emitted.
parts.markscheme:
- Explain mass-energy conversion: Mass is converted into binding energy of the nucleus (mass defect) <marks>R1</marks>
- Explain energy release: Energy carried away by the emitted electron and antineutrino also accounts for the loss <marks>R1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Recall mass-energy equivalence
steps.content:
In nuclear physics, mass and energy are not independent; they are equivalent. Einstein's mass-energy equivalence formula tells us that a change in mass corresponds to a specific amount of energy.

$$E = mc^2$$

This means that if a system loses mass, that mass has been converted into energy. In the context of $\beta^-$ decay, the total mass-energy of the system must be conserved.

steps.title: Account for energy release
steps.content:
For a decay to happen spontaneously, the products must be in a more stable, lower-energy state than the original isotope. The "missing" mass, often called the mass defect, is converted into the energy released during the decay process.

This energy is required to satisfy the conservation of energy. If the mass of the products (the daughter nucleus, the electron, and the antineutrino) were equal to the original mass, there would be no energy available for the particles to actually move!

highlights.text: mass of the product nucleus is less than the original mass

steps.title: Identify energy carriers
steps.content:
The energy equivalent of that lost mass ($Δm c^2$) is carried away as the **kinetic energy** of the decay products. 

Even though we only "see" the electron and antineutrino being emitted, the energy is shared among:
1. The kinetic energy of the emitted **electron**.
2. The kinetic energy of the **antineutrino**.
3. The **recoil kinetic energy** of the daughter (product) nucleus.

Because this energy is removed from the system by these particles, the remaining rest mass of the product nucleus must be lower than that of the parent nucleus.

highlights.text: only an electron and antineutrino are emitted

steps.title: Analyze nuclear stability
steps.content: Radioactive decay occurs because the parent nucleus is unstable. The resulting daughter (product) nucleus is **more stable** than the original parent nucleus. In nuclear physics, greater stability is characterized by a higher **binding energy per nucleon**.

steps.title: Apply mass-energy equivalence
steps.content:
Using the mass-energy equivalence formula from the data booklet:

$$E = mc^2$$

Binding energy is the energy equivalent of the **mass defect**—the difference between the mass of the individual nucleons and the actual mass of the nucleus. Because the product nucleus has a higher binding energy, it has a greater mass defect. This means a larger portion of its potential mass has been converted into energy, resulting in a **lower nuclear mass** compared to the parent.

steps.title: Account for energy release
steps.content: The total mass-energy of the system must be conserved. The difference in mass between the parent and the products (the mass loss) is released as energy. This energy is carried away as the kinetic energy of the emitted electron and antineutrino, further accounting for why the final mass of the product nucleus is less than the original mass.

highlights.text: mass of the product nucleus is less than the original mass

questions.specification:
Which of the following common types of radiation is most penetrating through matter?

Syllabus reference: 7.2.2

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic E - Nuclear and quantum physics > E.3 Radioactive decay > E.3.2 Types of radioactive decay
labeledSubSubTopics.title: E.3.2 Types of radioactive decay
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question compares alpha, beta, gamma (and neutron) for penetrating power through matter—content typically covered under types/properties of radioactive radiation.

options.markscheme: Alpha ($\alpha$) particles have the lowest penetrating power because of their relatively large mass and double positive charge. They are typically stopped by a single sheet of paper or a few centimeters of air.
Beta ($\beta$) particles have moderate penetrating power. They can pass through paper but are generally stopped by a few millimeters of aluminum foil.
Gamma ($\gamma$) rays are high-energy photons with no charge or rest mass. They have high penetrating power and require thick layers of lead or concrete to be significantly attenuated.

Neutron radiation consists of neutral particles that do not interact via the electromagnetic force. This makes them highly penetrating through most materials, often requiring thick hydrogen-rich shielding (like water or concrete) to be stopped. They are generally more penetrating than $\alpha$, $\beta$, and $\gamma$ radiation.

parts.content: Identify one source of background radiation.
parts.markscheme:
Cosmic rays / naturally occurring radioactive materials in rocks or air (e.g., Radon gas) <marks>B1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Define background radiation
steps.content: In IB Physics, **background radiation** refers to the ionizing radiation present in the environment that is not due to the deliberate introduction of a radioactive source. When a teacher uses a GM tube, it will always record a small number of counts even if no radioactive sample is nearby; this is known as the background count rate.

steps.title: Recall syllabus sources
steps.content:
According to **Topic E.3 (Radioactive decay)**, there are several standard natural and artificial sources of this radiation that you should be able to identify:

*   **Cosmic rays:** High-energy particles from outer space that enter the Earth's atmosphere.
*   **Radon gas:** A naturally occurring radioactive gas produced by the decay of uranium in rocks and soil.
*   **Terrestrial radiation:** Radioactive elements like thorium and uranium found naturally in the Earth's crust (rocks and soil).
*   **Internal radiation:** Radioactive isotopes (like Carbon-14 or Potassium-40) found inside biological organisms and food.

steps.title: Select a specific source
steps.content:
To earn the mark, you simply need to name one of these recognized sources. 

Commonly accepted answers include:
1.  **Cosmic rays**
2.  **Radon gas**
3.  **Radioactive rocks/soil**

Following the markscheme, we can identify **Radon gas** as a primary source.

highlights.text: Identify one source of background radiation.

parts.content: State the purpose of using a GM tube in radiation experiments.
parts.markscheme:
To detect ionising radiation and convert it into countable electrical signals <marks>B1</marks>

1 mark total

parts.content: Describe how the count rate measured by the GM tube can be used to determine the activity of a radioactive source.
parts.markscheme:
- Subtract background count rate from total measured count rate to find the corrected count rate <marks>B1</marks>
- Convert the corrected count rate to activity using known geometry, efficiency, or calibration factor <marks>B1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Calculate the corrected count rate
steps.content:
A GM tube detects both the radiation from the source and the naturally occurring background radiation. To find the amount of radiation coming *only* from the source, we first measure the background count rate with the source removed. 

We then subtract this background count rate from the total count rate measured when the source is present. This gives us the **corrected count rate**:

$$\text{Corrected Count Rate} = \text{Total Count Rate} - \text{Background Count Rate}$$

steps.title: Account for detector efficiency
steps.content:
The GM tube does not detect every single particle emitted by the source. This is because the detector only covers a small area around the source (geometry) and some particles may pass through the tube without being counted (efficiency).

To find the true **activity** (the total number of disintegrations per second), we divide the corrected count rate by a known **efficiency** or multiply it by a **calibration factor**. For example, if the detector efficiency is $10\%$, the activity would be:

$$\text{Activity} = \frac{\text{Corrected Count Rate}}{\text{Efficiency}}$$

steps.title: Summary for marks
steps.content:
In an IB exam, you earn the two marks by clearly stating these two procedural steps:
1.  **Subtract the background count rate** from the measured count rate to find the corrected count rate.
2.  **Scale the result** using a known calibration factor, geometry, or detector efficiency to determine the source's total activity.

steps.title: Subtract background radiation
steps.content:
To determine the radiation coming specifically from the source, we must first account for the environment. Measure the background count rate in the lab without the source present, then subtract this value from the total count rate measured with the source. This gives the **corrected count rate** ($R_c$):

$$R_c = \text{Total count rate} - \text{Background count rate}$$

steps.title: Calculate the geometric fraction
steps.content:
Radioactive sources typically emit radiation in all directions (isotropically) over the surface of an expanding sphere. A GM tube only detects the particles that enter its window. We calculate the fraction ($f$) of emitted particles that reach the detector by finding the ratio of the GM tube window area ($A$) to the surface area of a sphere with a radius equal to the distance ($d$) from the source:

$$f = \frac{A}{4\pi d^2}$$

steps.title: Determine total source activity
steps.content:
The **activity** is the total number of decays occurring in the source per second. Since the corrected count rate ($R_c$) only represents the particles entering the detector, we divide it by the geometric fraction ($f$) to find the total activity ($A_{source}$):

$$\text{Activity} = \frac{R_c}{f}$$

If the detector has a known efficiency (the percentage of particles entering the window that actually trigger a pulse), the count rate should also be divided by that efficiency factor.

parts.content: Suggest why repeated measurements are taken when measuring background radiation.
parts.markscheme:
To reduce the effect of random fluctuations / to reduce random error <marks>B1</marks>

1 mark total

parts.content: State what is meant by the term radioactive decay.
parts.markscheme:
- State the definition: The spontaneous and random transformation of an unstable nucleus into a more stable one, accompanied by the emission of radiation <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Identify the core process
steps.content: To define radioactive decay, we need to describe what is happening at the subatomic level. This is a process found in **Topic E.3: Radioactive decay**, where certain atomic nuclei are unstable and need to change to reach a more stable state.

steps.title: Define the nature of decay
steps.content:
In IB Physics, two specific words are essential when describing this process:

1. **Spontaneous**: The decay occurs naturally and is not affected by external factors like temperature or pressure.
2. **Random**: It is impossible to predict exactly which individual nucleus will decay or at what specific time it will happen.

steps.title: Describe the transformation
steps.content:
The process involves an **unstable nucleus** transforming into a **more stable nucleus**. 

During this transformation, the nucleus must lose energy or excess mass. It does this by releasing "radiation," which can take the form of particles (such as alpha or beta particles) or electromagnetic radiation (gamma rays).

steps.title: State the final definition
steps.content:
Combining these points, the formal definition required for the mark is:

**Radioactive decay is the spontaneous and random transformation of an unstable nucleus into a more stable one, accompanied by the emission of radiation.**

parts.content: Write an equation relating the number of undecayed nuclei $N$ to the initial number $N_0$ and the decay constant $\lambda$.
parts.markscheme:
- Write the equation: $N = N_0 e^{-\lambda t}$ <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Identify the decay relationship
steps.content:
In radioactive decay, the rate at which nuclei decay is proportional to the number of undecayed nuclei currently present in the sample. This relationship leads to an exponential decrease in the number of undecayed nuclei over time.

This concept is a core part of **Topic E.3: Radioactive decay** in the IB Physics HL syllabus.

steps.title: State the decay law
steps.content:
The **Radioactive Decay Law** expresses the number of undecayed nuclei $N$ remaining after a time $t$ as an exponential function. 

The equation is:

$$N = N_0 e^{-\lambda t}$$

Where:
- $N$ is the number of undecayed nuclei at time $t$
- $N_0$ is the initial number of undecayed nuclei
- $\lambda$ is the decay constant
- $t$ is the elapsed time

steps.title: Verify with a calculator
steps.content:
You can use your GDC to visualize this relationship or solve for specific values. 

For example, to find the fraction of nuclei remaining after a certain time, you would use the exponential function $e^x$. If you are given specific values for $\lambda$ and $t$, you can calculate the ratio $\frac{N}{N_0}$.

gdc.instructions: Go to the Run-Matrix menu. Press [SHIFT] [ln] to get e^. Type the negative product of the decay constant and time inside the parentheses.

ti84.instructions: To calculate e^(-λt), press [2nd] [ln] (which is the e^x function), then type -(decay_constant * time) and press [ENTER].

desmos.instructions: Type 'exp(-lambda * t)' or 'e^(-lambda * t)' into the expression bar, replacing lambda and t with your known values.

calculator.description: Calculate the fraction of nuclei remaining given decay constant and time.

parts.content: Calculate the activity of a sample containing $2.0 \times 10^6$ nuclei with a decay constant $\lambda = 1.5 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{s}^{-1}$.
parts.markscheme:
- Use the activity equation $A = \lambda N$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Calculate the final answer: $A = 1.5 \times 10^{-4} \times 2.0 \times 10^6 = 300\ \text{Bq}$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify the given values
steps.content:
First, let's identify the information provided in the question. This topic falls under **Topic E.3: Radioactive decay**.

*   The number of undecayed nuclei is $N = 2.0 \times 10^6$.
*   The decay constant is $\lambda = 1.5 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{s}^{-1}$.

steps.title: Recall the activity formula
steps.content:
The activity ($A$) of a sample is the rate at which nuclei decay. It is directly proportional to the number of undecayed nuclei present ($N$) and the decay constant ($\lambda$). 

The fundamental relationship is:

$$A = \lambda N$$

In an IB exam, showing this formula earns you the first mark (**M1**).

steps.title: Calculate the activity
steps.content:
Now, we substitute the given values into the equation to find the activity in Becquerels (Bq), which represents decays per second:

$$\begin{aligned} A &= (1.5 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{s}^{-1}) \times (2.0 \times 10^6) \\ A &= 300\ \text{Bq} \end{aligned}$$

Correctly calculating this value earns the second mark (**A1**).

ti84.instructions: Enter the product using scientific notation: 1.5E-4 * 2.0E6

desmos.instructions: Type the expression directly into the cell.

calculator.description: Calculate the activity by multiplying the decay constant and the number of nuclei.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content: The activity of the sample is **300 Bq**.

parts.content: Determine the number of undecayed nuclei remaining after 1 hour.
parts.markscheme:
- Substitute values into the decay equation using $t = 3600\ \text{s}$: $N = 2.0 \times 10^6 \times e^{-1.5 \times 10^{-4} \times 3600}$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Calculate the final answer: $N = 1.2 \times 10^6$ (accept $1.17 \times 10^6$) <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Convert time to seconds
steps.content:
To use the radioactive decay law, the units for time ($t$) must match the units for the decay constant ($\lambda$). Since $\lambda$ is usually given in $\text{s}^{-1}$, we convert 1 hour into seconds.

$$1 \text{ hour} = 60 \times 60 = 3600 \text{ s}$$

steps.title: State the decay law
steps.content:
In **Topic E.3: Radioactive decay**, we learn that the number of undecayed nuclei decreases exponentially over time according to the formula:

$$N = N_0 e^{-\lambda t}$$

Where:
- $N$ is the number of remaining nuclei
- $N_0$ is the initial number of nuclei ($2.0 \times 10^6$)
- $\lambda$ is the decay constant ($1.5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s}^{-1}$)
- $t$ is the time elapsed in seconds

steps.title: Substitute and solve
steps.content:
Substitute the values into the equation to calculate the remaining nuclei. This step earns you the first mark (**M1**).

$$\begin{aligned} N &= (2.0 \times 10^6) \times e^{-(1.5 \times 10^{-4} \times 3600)} \\ N &= (2.0 \times 10^6) \times e^{-0.54} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter the expression into the main calculation screen. Ensure the entire exponent is inside parentheses.

ti84.instructions: Enter 2.0E6 * e^(-1.5E-4 * 3600). Use the [2nd] [LN] keys for the 'e^' function.

desmos.instructions: Type the formula directly into the expression bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the final number of nuclei using the exponential function.

steps.title: Final Answer
steps.content:
Using your calculator, evaluate the expression to find the number of undecayed nuclei. Rounding to two significant figures, we get:

$$N = 1.2 \times 10^6$$

This final value earns the second mark (**A1**).

steps.title: Convert time to seconds
steps.content:
To ensure our units are consistent with the decay constant $\lambda$ (which is in $\text{s}^{-1}$), we first convert the time from hours to seconds.

$$1\text{ hour} = 60 \times 60 = 3600\text{ s}$$

steps.title: Calculate the half-life
steps.content:
Using the decay constant $\lambda = 1.5 \times 10^{-4}\text{ s}^{-1}$, we calculate the half-life ($T_{1/2}$) of the isotope:

$$T_{1/2} = \frac{\ln(2)}{\lambda}$$

Substituting the values:

$$T_{1/2} = \frac{0.693}{1.5 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 4621\text{ s}$$

gdc.instructions: Use the log function (natural log) to calculate ln(2) / (1.5E-4)

calculator.description: Calculate the half-life using the decay constant.

steps.title: Determine elapsed half-lives
steps.content:
Next, we find out how many half-lives ($n$) have passed in $3600\text{ s}$ by dividing the total time by the half-life:

$$n = \frac{t}{T_{1/2}}$$

$$n = \frac{3600}{4621} \approx 0.779$$

calculator.description: Calculate the number of half-lives that have passed.

steps.title: Calculate remaining nuclei
steps.content:
Finally, we use the initial number of nuclei $N_0 = 2.0 \times 10^6$ and the number of half-lives $n$ to find the remaining nuclei ($N$):

$$N = N_0 \times (0.5)^n$$

$$N = (2.0 \times 10^6) \times (0.5)^{0.779}$$

$$N \approx 1.165 \times 10^6$$

calculator.description: Calculate the final number of undecayed nuclei.

steps.title: State final answer
steps.content:
Rounding to two significant figures, as is standard in IB Physics when the inputs have two significant figures, we get:

$$N = 1.2 \times 10^6$$

parts.content: Suggest why the decay of a single unstable nucleus cannot be predicted, but the decay of a large number of nuclei can be described statistically.
parts.markscheme:
- Explain the nature of decay: The decay of a specific nucleus is random and cannot be predicted, but with many nuclei the behaviour averages out and follows predictable laws (exponential decay) <marks>B1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Analyze individual nucleus behavior
steps.content: Radioactive decay is a **spontaneous** and **random** process. For any **single unstable nucleus**, there is no way to determine exactly when it will decay; we only know that it has a constant probability of decaying per unit of time.

highlights.text: single unstable nucleus cannot be predicted

steps.title: Apply statistical averaging
steps.content: When we transition from one nucleus to a **large number of nuclei**, the random fluctuations of individuals start to average out. Because each nucleus has the same constant probability of decay, the overall rate of decay for the entire sample becomes highly predictable.

highlights.text: decay of a large number of nuclei

steps.title: Formulate the final explanation
steps.content: In physics, this transition from individual randomness to collective certainty is called **statistical behavior**. While we can't predict one event, the massive number of atoms in a typical sample ensures that the group follows a precise mathematical law, specifically **exponential decay**.

parts.content: A radioactive sample has an initial activity of $400 \text{ Bq}$ at time $t = 0$. After $12$ hours, the activity has decreased to $50 \text{ Bq}$. Determine the activity of the sample when $t = 16$ hours.
parts.markscheme:
$12$ hours represents $3$ half-lives (since $400 \rightarrow 200 \rightarrow 100 \rightarrow 50$), so the half-life $T_{1/2}$ is $4$ hours <marks> 1 mark </marks>

at $t = 16$ hours, $4$ half-lives have passed, so activity $= 400 \times (\frac{1}{2})^4 = 25 \text{ Bq}$ <marks> 1 mark </marks>

parts.content:
The alpha decay of Americium-241 is represented by the following masses:

- Mass of ${}_{95}^{241}\mathrm{Am} = 241.0047 \text{ u}$
- Mass of ${}_{93}^{237}\mathrm{Np} = 236.9971 \text{ u}$
- Mass of ${}_{2}^{4}\alpha = 4.0015 \text{ u}$

Determine the total energy released in this decay in Joules $(\text{J})$.

Data:
$1 \text{ u} = 931.5 \text{ MeV}\,c^{-2}$
$1 \text{ eV} = 1.60 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}$
parts.markscheme:
total mass of products $= 236.9971 + 4.0015 = 240.9986 \text{ u}$ <marks> 1 mark </marks>

mass defect $\Delta m = 241.0047 - 240.9986 = 0.0061 \text{ u}$ <marks> 1 mark </marks>

energy in $\text{MeV} = 0.0061 \times 931.5 = 5.68 \text{ MeV}$ <marks> 1 mark </marks>

energy in $\text{J} = 5.68 \times 10^{6} \times 1.60 \times 10^{-19} = 9.09 \times 10^{-13} \text{ J}$ <marks> 1 mark </marks>

*Accept answers in the range $9.0 \times 10^{-13} \text{ J}$ to $9.2 \times 10^{-13} \text{ J}$ depending on intermediate rounding.*

The initial activities are $A_X = 800\ \text{Bq}$ and $A_Y = 1200\ \text{Bq}$. The half-lives are $T_{1/2,X} = 1.0\ \text{hour}$ and $T_{1/2,Y} = 3.0\ \text{hours}$.
