parts.content: Calculate the decay constant of the isotope.
parts.markscheme:
- Substitute the half-life into the decay constant formula: $\lambda = \frac{\ln 2}{T_{1/2}} = \frac{0.693}{2.5 \times 86400}$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Calculate the final value: $\lambda \approx 3.21 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{s}^{-1}$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Convert half-life to seconds
steps.content:
To find the decay constant ($\lambda$) in standard SI units ($\text{s}^{-1}$), we first need to convert the half-life from days into seconds. There are 24 hours in a day, and 3600 seconds in an hour.

$$\begin{aligned} T_{1/2} &= 2.5 \text{ days} \\ T_{1/2} &= 2.5 \times 24 \times 3600 \\ T_{1/2} &= 216,000 \text{ s} \end{aligned}$$

This is a necessary step because decay constants are typically expressed in reciprocal seconds in IB Physics.

calculator.description: Calculate the total seconds in 2.5 days.

steps.title: Apply decay constant formula
steps.content:
Next, we use the fundamental relationship between the decay constant and the half-life. This relationship is covered in **Topic E.3: Radioactive decay**. The formula is:

$$\lambda = \frac{\ln 2}{T_{1/2}}$$

Substituting our values:

$$\lambda = \frac{\ln 2}{216,000}$$

Note that $\ln 2$ is approximately $0.693$. In the IB markscheme, using either $\ln 2$ or $0.693$ is acceptable.

steps.title: Calculate final value
steps.content:
Now, perform the final calculation to find the value of $\lambda$.

$$\begin{aligned} \lambda &= \frac{0.6931...}{216,000} \\ \lambda &\approx 3.21 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

According to the markscheme, you receive one mark (M1) for the correct substitution and one mark (A1) for the correct final answer with units.

gdc.instructions: Calculate natural log of 2 divided by 216000.

desmos.instructions: Enter ln(2) / 216000 into the expression box.

calculator.description: Calculate the final decay constant.

steps.title: Convert half-life to seconds
steps.content:
To calculate the decay constant in SI units ($\text{s}^{-1}$), we first need to convert the half-life from days to seconds. Since there are 24 hours in a day, 60 minutes in an hour, and 60 seconds in a minute:

$$T_{1/2} = 2.5 \times 24 \times 60 \times 60$$
$$T_{1/2} = 216,000\text{ s}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the total number of seconds in 2.5 days.

steps.title: Set up decay equation
steps.content:
The general radioactive decay law is given by:

$$N = N_0 e^{-\lambda t}$$

By definition, after one half-life ($t = T_{1/2}$), the number of remaining atoms $N$ is exactly half of the initial amount ($N = 0.5 N_0$). Substituting these into the formula, we get:

$$0.5 N_0 = N_0 e^{-\lambda T_{1/2}}$$
$$0.5 = e^{-\lambda (216,000)}$$

steps.title: Solve using logarithms
steps.content:
To solve for the decay constant $\lambda$, we take the natural logarithm ($\ln$) of both sides of the equation:

$$\ln(0.5) = -\lambda (216,000)$$

Rearranging for $\lambda$:

$$\lambda = \frac{\ln(0.5)}{-216,000}$$

Since $\ln(0.5) = -\ln(2)$, this simplifies to the standard formula:

$$\lambda = \frac{\ln 2}{216,000}$$

ti84.instructions: Press the [ln] key, type 2, close parentheses, then press [/] and type 216000. Press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Type ln(2) / 216000 into the input bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the final value of the decay constant.

steps.title: Final Result
steps.content:
Using the calculator, we find the decay constant:

$$\lambda \approx 3.21 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{s}^{-1}$$

This represents the probability of a single nucleus decaying per second. In the IB markscheme, you earn one mark for the correct substitution and one mark for the final value with units.

steps.title: Identify the Decay Formula
steps.content:
To find the decay constant ($\lambda$), we use the relationship between half-life ($T_{1/2}$) and the decay constant. This formula is derived from the exponential decay law found in **Topic E.3: Radioactive decay**.

$$\lambda = \frac{\ln 2}{T_{1/2}}$$

Here, the half-life is given in days, so our initial calculation will give us the decay constant per day.

steps.title: Calculate Lambda per Day
steps.content:
Substitute the value of the half-life into the formula. Since the half-life is $2.5\text{ days}$, we calculate:

$$\lambda = \frac{\ln 2}{2.5}$$

$$\lambda \approx 0.2773\text{ day}^{-1}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the decay constant in days⁻¹.

steps.title: Find Seconds per Day
steps.content:
To convert the unit from $\text{day}^{-1}$ to $\text{s}^{-1}$ later, we first need to know how many seconds are in one full day.

$$\text{1 day} = 24\text{ hours} \times 60\text{ minutes} \times 60\text{ seconds}$$

$$\text{1 day} = 86,400\text{ s}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the number of seconds in a day.

steps.title: Convert to Final Units
steps.content:
Finally, convert $\lambda$ from $\text{day}^{-1}$ to $\text{s}^{-1}$ by dividing the daily decay constant by the total number of seconds in a day.

$$\lambda_{\text{s}^{-1}} = \frac{\lambda_{\text{day}^{-1}}}{86,400}$$

$$\lambda = \frac{0.27725...}{86,400} \approx 3.21 \times 10^{-6}\text{ s}^{-1}$$

This matches the requirement for the decay constant in standard SI units.

calculator.description: Convert the decay constant to per second units.

parts.content: Determine the number of undecayed nuclei remaining after 7.0 days.
parts.markscheme:
- Convert the time interval to seconds: $t = 7.0 \times 86400 = 604800\ \text{s}$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Substitute into the decay equation to find $N$: $N = N_0 e^{-\lambda t} = 6.0 \times 10^{18} \times e^{-(3.21 \times 10^{-6})(604800)} \approx 8.7 \times 10^{17}$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

<note>Accept $8.6 \times 10^{17}$ if unrounded values are used.</note>

steps.title: Identify knowns and convert units
steps.content:
To use the exponential decay law, we first need to ensure our time values are in the same units (typically seconds, as preferred in IB Physics exams).

From the question, we have:
- Initial number of atoms: $N_0 = 6.0 \times 10^{18}$ atoms
- Half-life: $T_{1/2} = 2.5$ days
- Time elapsed: $t = 7.0$ days

Let's convert the time interval $t$ to seconds:
$$t = 7.0 \text{ days} \times 24 \frac{\text{h}}{\text{day}} \times 3600 \frac{\text{s}}{\text{h}} = 604,800 \text{ s}$$

steps.title: Calculate the decay constant
steps.content:
The decay constant ($\lambda$) represents the probability of decay per unit time. We can calculate it using the relationship found in the data booklet:

$$\lambda = \frac{\ln 2}{T_{1/2}}$$

First, convert the half-life to seconds:
$$T_{1/2} = 2.5 \times 86400 = 216,000 \text{ s}$$

Now, calculate $\lambda$:
$$\lambda = \frac{\ln 2}{216,000} \approx 3.21 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s}^{-1}$$

steps.title: Apply the decay law
steps.content:
Now we substitute our values into the radioactive decay law formula to find the remaining nuclei $N$:

$$N = N_0 e^{-\lambda t}$$

Substituting our values:
$$N = (6.0 \times 10^{18}) \times e^{-(3.21 \times 10^{-6} \times 604,800)}$$

Calculating the exponent first:
$$-(3.21 \times 10^{-6} \times 604,800) \approx -1.941$$

Finally:
$$N = 6.0 \times 10^{18} \times e^{-1.941} \approx 8.6 \times 10^{17}$$

steps.title: Verify using a calculator
steps.content: Using a graphing calculator (GDC) is the most efficient way to handle these large exponents and scientific notation precisely.

gdc.instructions: In the Run-Matrix menu, enter the initial quantity, the exponential constant, and the product of decay constant and time.

ti84.instructions: Enter the expression using the [2nd] [EE] key for scientific notation and [2nd] [e^x] for the exponential function.

desmos.instructions: Type the formula using the 'exp' function or 'e^'. You can define the constants separately for clarity.

calculator.description: Calculate the final number of nuclei $N$ directly using the decay law.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The number of undecayed nuclei remaining after 7.0 days is approximately:

$$N = 8.6 \times 10^{17} \text{ atoms}$$

(Note: The markscheme also accepts $8.7 \times 10^{17}$ depending on intermediate rounding of $\lambda$. Both are mathematically acceptable for full marks.)

steps.title: Identify given values
steps.content:
To find the number of undecayed nuclei remaining, we first list the information given in the question:
- Initial number of atoms ($N_0$): $6.0 \times 10^{18}$ atoms
- Half-life ($T_{1/2}$): $2.5$ days
- Total time elapsed ($t$): $7.0$ days

This question is covered in **Topic E.3: Radioactive Decay**. We are looking for the remaining quantity $N$.

steps.title: Calculate elapsed half-lives
steps.content:
First, we determine how many half-lives have passed during the 7.0-day interval. We use the formula:

$$n = \frac{t}{T_{1/2}}$$

Substituting our values:

$$n = \frac{7.0 \text{ days}}{2.5 \text{ days}} = 2.8 \text{ half-lives}$$

desmos.instructions: Input 7.0 / 2.5 in a new expression line.

calculator.description: Calculate the number of half-lives.

steps.title: Apply the decay formula
steps.content:
Now we can find the remaining number of nuclei ($N$) by using the exponential decay relationship based on the number of half-lives:

$$N = N_0 \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^n$$

Substituting $N_0 = 6.0 \times 10^{18}$ and $n = 2.8$:

$$N = (6.0 \times 10^{18}) \times (0.5)^{2.8}$$

gdc.instructions: Multiply 6E18 by 0.5 raised to the power of 2.8.

calculator.description: Calculate the final number of undecayed nuclei.

steps.title: State final answer
steps.content:
Using the calculation from the previous step:

$$N \approx 8.615 \times 10^{17}$$

Since our input values ($6.0$, $2.5$, $7.0$) are given to two significant figures, we should round our final answer to two significant figures as well.

$$N = 8.6 \times 10^{17} \text{ nuclei}$$

parts.content: Calculate the total number of decays that occurred during this period.
parts.markscheme:
- Calculate the difference between initial and remaining nuclei: $N_{\text{decayed}} = N_0 - N = 6.0 \times 10^{18} - 8.7 \times 10^{17} = 5.13 \times 10^{18}$ <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

<note>Accept $5.14 \times 10^{18}$ if consistent with previous part.</note>

steps.title: Identify the given information
steps.content:
To begin, let's list the known values and ensure our units for time are consistent. The half-life is given in days, so we should convert the one-week period into days as well.

*   Initial number of atoms: $N_0 = 6.0 \times 10^{18}$
*   Half-life: $T_{1/2} = 2.5$ days
*   Time elapsed ($t$): 1 week = $7$ days

steps.title: Calculate the decay constant
steps.content:
Using the formula for the decay constant $\lambda$ from the Nuclear Physics section of the data booklet:

$$\lambda = \frac{\ln(2)}{T_{1/2}}$$

Substituting our values:
$$\lambda = \frac{\ln(2)}{2.5} \approx 0.27726 \text{ days}^{-1}$$

steps.title: Determine remaining nuclei
steps.content:
Next, we use the exponential decay law to calculate the number of nuclei remaining ($N$) after 7 days:

$$N = N_0 e^{-\lambda t}$$

Substituting the values:
$$N = (6.0 \times 10^{18}) \times e^{-(0.27726 \times 7)}$$
$$N \approx 8.62 \times 10^{17}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter the exponential decay formula directly.

ti84.instructions: Use the 'e^x' and 'ln' functions for precision.

calculator.description: Calculate the number of remaining nuclei after 7 days.

steps.title: Calculate total decays
steps.content:
The total number of decays is the difference between the starting number of nuclei and the number of nuclei left over:

$$\text{Total decays} = N_0 - N$$

Using the value for $N$ calculated previously (or the rounded value $8.7 \times 10^{17}$ often used in these problems):
$$\text{Total decays} = 6.0 \times 10^{18} - 8.7 \times 10^{17} = 5.13 \times 10^{18}$$

highlights.text: Calculate the difference between initial and remaining nuclei: $N_{\text{decayed}} = N_0 - N$

steps.title: Define time and half-life
steps.content:
To solve this using the half-life ratio approach, we first need to express the total elapsed time ($t$) and the half-life ($T_{1/2}$) in the same units. The question states the evaluation period is "over a week."

- Total time ($t$) = 7 days
- Half-life ($T_{1/2}$) = 2.5 days

steps.title: Calculate number of half-lives
steps.content:
Next, we calculate how many half-lives ($n$) have passed during this 7-day period using the ratio:

$$n = \frac{t}{T_{1/2}}$$

$$n = \frac{7.0}{2.5} = 2.8$$

This means the source has undergone 2.8 half-lives.

calculator.description: Calculate the ratio of total time to half-life.

steps.title: Find nuclei remaining
steps.content:
We can now find the number of nuclei remaining ($N$) using the decay formula based on the number of half-lives:

$$N = N_0 \times (0.5)^n$$

Substituting our values:

$$N = (6.0 \times 10^{18}) \times (0.5)^{2.8}$$
$$N \approx 8.62 \times 10^{17}$$ atoms

*(Note: Depending on previous rounding, this value might be given as $8.7 \times 10^{17}$ in other parts of the question.)*

gdc.instructions: Multiply the initial count by 0.5 raised to the power of 2.8

calculator.description: Calculate the remaining number of nuclei.

steps.title: Calculate total decays
steps.content:
The number of decays is the difference between the initial number of atoms and the remaining number of atoms:

$$N_{\text{decayed}} = N_0 - N$$

Using the values from the previous steps (and following the markscheme's rounding for $N$):

$$N_{\text{decayed}} = 6.0 \times 10^{18} - 8.7 \times 10^{17}$$
$$N_{\text{decayed}} = 5.13 \times 10^{18}$$

This represents the total number of alpha or beta particles (in this case, $\beta^-$) emitted by the source over the week.

gdc.instructions: Subtract the values using scientific notation

calculator.description: Subtract the remaining nuclei from the initial population.

steps.title: Identify the total time
steps.content:
The question asks for the number of decays that occur over a "week". To use the decay formulas correctly, we must ensure our time units match the half-life units (days).

$$t = 7 \text{ days}$$

steps.title: Calculate number of half-lives
steps.content:
First, let's find how many half-lives ($n$) have passed during these 7 days. We divide the total time by the half-life given in the problem:

$$n = \frac{t}{T_{1/2}}$$

$$n = \frac{7}{2.5} = 2.8$$

desmos.instructions: Type 7/2.5 to find the number of half-lives.

calculator.description: Calculate the number of half-lives elapsed.

steps.title: Determine the decay fraction
steps.content:
Using the **Direct Decay Fraction** approach, we find what portion of the original sample has decayed. 

If the fraction remaining is $(0.5)^n$, then the fraction that has **decayed** is:
$$\text{Fraction decayed} = 1 - (0.5)^n$$

Substituting our value for $n$:
$$\text{Fraction decayed} = 1 - (0.5)^{2.8} \approx 0.8564$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate 1 minus 0.5 raised to the power of 2.8.

desmos.instructions: Type 1 - 0.5^{2.8} to see the decayed fraction.

calculator.description: Calculate the fraction of atoms that have decayed.

steps.title: Calculate total decays
steps.content:
Finally, we multiply the initial number of atoms ($N_0$) by the fraction that decayed to find the total number of decays:

$$N_{\text{decayed}} = N_0 \times (\text{Fraction decayed})$$

$$N_{\text{decayed}} = 6.0 \times 10^{18} \times 0.8564...$$

$$N_{\text{decayed}} \approx 5.14 \times 10^{18}$$

gdc.instructions: Multiply the initial number of atoms by the fraction calculated in the previous step.

calculator.description: Find the total number of decays by multiplying the initial amount by the decay fraction.

steps.title: Final Result
steps.content:
The total number of decays during the one-week period is approximately $5.14 \times 10^{18}$. 

In IB exams, you may see slight variations in the last decimal place (like $5.13 \times 10^{18}$) depending on whether values from previous parts of the question were rounded differently. Both are generally accepted if your working is shown clearly.

parts.content: Determine the average activity of the sample over the 7.0-day interval.
parts.markscheme:
- Substitute values into the average activity formula: $\text{Average activity} = \frac{N_{\text{decayed}}}{t} = \frac{5.13 \times 10^{18}}{604800}$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Calculate the final activity: $8.48 \times 10^{12}\ \text{Bq}$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

<note>Accept $8.5 \times 10^{12}\ \text{Bq}$ if consistent with previous part.</note>

steps.title: Define average activity
steps.content:
Average activity is defined as the total number of nuclei that decay ($\Delta N$) divided by the time interval ($\Delta t$) over which the decay occurred. 

$$\text{Average Activity} = \frac{\Delta N}{\Delta t}$$

To find this, we first need to determine how many atoms decayed during the 7.0-day period. This is the difference between the initial number of atoms and the atoms remaining: $\Delta N = N_0 - N$.

steps.title: Calculate the decay constant
steps.content:
Using the **Decay Constant and Exponential Law** approach, we first find the decay constant ($\lambda$). The relationship between the decay constant and the half-life ($T_{1/2}$) is given by:

$$\lambda = \frac{\ln 2}{T_{1/2}}$$

Given that the half-life is 2.5 days:

$$\lambda = \frac{\ln 2}{2.5} \approx 0.277 \text{ day}^{-1}$$

steps.title: Find remaining atoms
steps.content:
Now we use the exponential decay law to find the number of atoms remaining ($N$) after $t = 7.0$ days:

$$N = N_0 e^{-\lambda t}$$

Substituting the values:

$$N = (6.0 \times 10^{18}) \times e^{-(0.277 \times 7.0)}$$
$$N \approx 8.62 \times 10^{17} \text{ atoms}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the remaining number of atoms using the exponential decay formula.

steps.title: Determine decayed atoms
steps.content:
The number of nuclei that decayed ($\Delta N$) is the difference between the initial and final populations:

$$\Delta N = N_0 - N$$
$$\Delta N = 6.0 \times 10^{18} - 8.62 \times 10^{17}$$
$$\Delta N \approx 5.138 \times 10^{18} \text{ atoms}$$

steps.title: Convert time and calculate
steps.content:
Activity is usually expressed in Becquerels (Bq), which is decays per **second**. We must convert the 7.0-day interval into seconds:

$$\Delta t = 7.0 \times 24 \times 60 \times 60 = 604,800 \text{ s}$$

Now, substitute the values into the average activity formula:

$$\text{Average Activity} = \frac{5.138 \times 10^{18}}{604,800}$$
$$\text{Average Activity} \approx 8.50 \times 10^{12} \text{ Bq}$$

desmos.instructions: Divide the number of decayed atoms by the time in seconds.

calculator.description: Calculate the final average activity.

steps.title: Final Result
steps.content:
The average activity of the sample over the 7.0-day interval is approximately:

$$\text{Average Activity} = 8.5 \times 10^{12} \text{ Bq}$$

(Note: The markscheme also accepts $8.48 \times 10^{12} \text{ Bq}$ based on slight rounding differences during the calculation.)

steps.title: Identify given parameters
steps.content:
To find the average activity, we need to know the initial number of nuclei, the half-life, and the total time elapsed. This question relates to **Topic E.3: Radioactive decay**.

- Initial nuclei: $N_0 = 6.0 \times 10^{18}$
- Half-life: $T_{1/2} = 2.5$ days
- Time interval: $t = 7.0$ days

steps.title: Calculate half-lives elapsed
steps.content:
First, we determine the number of half-lives ($n$) that have passed during the 7.0-day interval:

$$n = \frac{t}{T_{1/2}} = \frac{7.0}{2.5} = 2.8$$

calculator.description: Calculate the number of half-lives elapsed.

steps.title: Find remaining atoms
steps.content:
Now, use the proportion formula to find the number of atoms remaining ($N$) after 2.8 half-lives:

$$N = N_0 \times (0.5)^n$$
$$N = 6.0 \times 10^{18} \times (0.5)^{2.8}$$
$$N \approx 8.615 \times 10^{17}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the remaining number of atoms.

steps.title: Calculate decayed atoms
steps.content:
The number of decayed atoms ($N_{\text{decayed}}$) is the initial amount minus the remaining amount:

$$N_{\text{decayed}} = N_0 - N$$
$$N_{\text{decayed}} = 6.0 \times 10^{18} - 8.615 \times 10^{17} \approx 5.138 \times 10^{18}$$

calculator.description: Subtract the remaining atoms from the initial atoms.

steps.title: Convert time to seconds
steps.content:
Activity is decays per second ($Bq$). Convert the 7.0-day interval to seconds:

$$t = 7.0 \text{ days} \times 24 \text{ h/day} \times 3600 \text{ s/h} = 604,800 \text{ s}$$

calculator.description: Calculate total seconds in 7 days.

steps.title: Calculate average activity
steps.content:
The average activity is the total number of decays divided by the total time:

$$\text{Average activity} = \frac{N_{\text{decayed}}}{t}$$
$$\text{Average activity} = \frac{5.138 \times 10^{18}}{604,800} \approx 8.50 \times 10^{12} \text{ Bq}$$

parts.content: Explain how the shielding effectiveness could be assessed using the measurements from the detector, considering the exponential decay.
parts.markscheme:
- State that expected count rates (without shielding) are calculated using the decay law <marks>A1</marks>

- Compare measured count rates with expected rates; lower measured rates indicate effective shielding <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Calculate the expected count rate
steps.content:
To assess the shield, we first need a baseline of what the radiation level *should* be without interference. Using the radioactive decay law, we calculate the expected activity (or count rate) for any time $t$ during the week. 

First, we find the decay constant $\lambda$ from the half-life:
$$\lambda = \frac{\ln(2)}{t_{1/2}}$$

Then, we use the exponential decay equation to find the expected count rate ($A_{\text{expected}}$):
$$A_{\text{expected}} = A_0 e^{-\lambda t}$$

This tells us the theoretical number of $\beta^-$ particles reaching the detector if the shielding were not present.

highlights.text: expected count rates (without shielding) are calculated using the decay law

steps.title: Verify theoretical values
steps.content: Let's use a calculator to see how the activity should drop over time. For example, if we want to know the expected activity after 5 days (exactly two half-lives), we can calculate the remaining fraction.

gdc.instructions: Enter the exponential decay formula: 0.5^(t / 2.5) where t = 5.

ti84.instructions: Calculate 0.5 raised to the power of (time / half-life).

desmos.instructions: Type the expression to find the remaining fraction.

calculator.description: Calculate the fraction of activity remaining after 5 days given a 2.5-day half-life.

steps.title: Compare measured vs. expected
steps.content:
Once we have the theoretical values, we compare them to the actual **measured count rates** recorded by the detector. 

If the shielding is effective, the measured count rate will be significantly **lower** than the expected count rate calculated in the previous step. The difference between these two values represents the radiation absorbed or blocked by the shielding material.

highlights.text: Compare measured count rates with expected rates; lower measured rates indicate effective shielding

steps.title: Final assessment of effectiveness
steps.content: The effectiveness is evaluated by the degree of attenuation. If the measured activity follows the exponential decay curve but remains at a consistently lower magnitude than the 'no-shield' prediction, the material is successfully reducing the $\beta^-$ radiation exposure.

steps.title: Model the theoretical decay
steps.content:
To assess shielding, we first need to know what the detector *should* record if there were no shielding at all. We use the radioactive decay law to calculate the expected count rates over the 7-day period. 

Since we know the **initial $N_0$** and the **half-life of 2.5 days**, we can model the decay using:

$$N = N_0 e^{-\lambda t}$$

where the decay constant $\lambda = \frac{\ln 2}{t_{1/2}}$. This gives us a theoretical curve representing the natural decrease in activity over time.

steps.title: Plot experimental measurements
steps.content: Next, we take the actual measurements recorded by the detector over the week. These represent the radiation that successfully passed through the shielding material. We plot these measured count rates as data points on the same axes as our theoretical curve.

steps.title: Compare curves to assess shielding
steps.content:
Finally, we compare the two curves. Because the theoretical curve represents a zero-shielding scenario, any effective shielding will result in the experimental curve sitting **below** the theoretical one. 

If the measured count rates are significantly lower than the expected rates, it indicates that the shielding material is effectively absorbing or blocking the $\beta^-$ particles. The larger the gap between the two curves, the more effective the shielding.

gdc.instructions: Graph the function f(x) = 0.5^(x/2.5) and evaluate it at x = 7.

ti84.instructions: Go to Y= and enter Y1 = 0.5^(X/2.5). Go to TABLE (2nd + GRAPH) to see the fraction of source remaining at X=7 days.

calculator.description: Visualizing the decay fraction after 1 week (7 days).

highlights.text: Compare measured count rates with expected rates; lower measured rates indicate effective shielding

steps.title: Calculate expected total decays
steps.content:
To assess shielding effectiveness, we first determine how many radioactive atoms are expected to decay during the one-week period ($t = 7$ days). Using the radioactive decay law from **Topic E.3**, we calculate the number of atoms remaining ($N$) and then find the change in the number of atoms ($\Delta N$).

The number of remaining atoms is given by:

$$N = N_0 \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{\frac{t}{T_{1/2}}}$$

The total number of expected decays is:

$$\Delta N = N_0 - N$$

This value represents the total number of $\beta^-$ particles emitted by the source if there were no shielding and 100% detection efficiency.

gdc.instructions: Use the exponential decay formula to find the total change in atoms.

ti84.instructions: Enter the initial number of atoms and multiply by the fraction that decays.

desmos.instructions: Define the parameters and calculate the difference.

calculator.description: Calculate the total expected decays ($ΔN$) over 7 days.

steps.title: Compare measured with expected
steps.content:
Next, we sum the counts recorded by the detector over the entire week to get the total measured count. 

We then compare this **measured total** to the **expected total decays** ($\Delta N$). 

If the shielding is effective, the detector will record significantly fewer counts than the calculated $\Delta N$. This is because the shielding material absorbs or deflects the $\beta^-$ particles before they can reach the detector.

highlights.text: State that expected count rates (without shielding) are calculated using the decay law
Compare measured count rates with expected rates; lower measured rates indicate effective shielding

steps.title: Evaluate shielding effectiveness
steps.content:
The effectiveness is quantified by the ratio of measured counts to expected decays. 

- A very low ratio indicates highly effective shielding.
- If the measured counts follow the expected exponential decay curve but at a much lower magnitude, it confirms the shielding is consistently blocking a specific percentage of radiation.

questions.specification:
An atom has four discrete energy levels. The energy of each level is given in the table below:

| Energy Level | Energy / eV |
| :--- | :--- |
| $$n=4$$ | $$-0.8$$ |
| $$n=3$$ | $$-2.2$$ |
| $$n=2$$ | $$-4.6$$ |
| $$n=1$$ | $$-12.4$$ |

How many distinct lines in the emission spectrum of this atom correspond to a photon energy greater than $$6.0 \text{ eV}$$?
